title: Vaughters says night tests would have saved cycling '25 years of drama'
text:
Former professional cyclist and Cannondale-EF team boss Jonathan Vaughters has weighed in on the row over pre-dawn doping tests at the Tour de France, arguing such controls ​could have exposed cycling's blood-doping era decades earlier had they existed at the time.

Riders including race leader Tadej Pogačar and Jonas Vingegaard were woken in the early hours of Sunday for unannounced doping checks ahead of ​the 15th stage, prompting ‌complaints that the disruption to sleep was unfair and potentially dangerous.

Vaughters, who rode professionally through the late 1990s and early 2000s, defended the practice.

"2am testing is one of the very effective ways ‌to prevent peptide (EPO, hGH) and testosterone micro dosing," Vaughters wrote on social media.

"If ⁠UCI (Union Cycliste Internationale) had tested us in 2001 at 2am? We'd all have ‌been suspended and saved the sport ​25 years of drama."

His comments ‌carry particular weight given his own history with doping.

Jonathan Vaughters (right) was teammates with Lance Armstrong (left) on the US Postal team. (Getty Images: Graham Watson)

Vaughters was a teammate of Lance Armstrong on the US Postal Service team and later admitted using performance-enhancing drugs, including EPO, during his career.

When Lance met Oprah Lance Armstrong had successfully doped for years. But when the evidence backed him into a corner, he took control of the narrative and confessed live on Oprah's couch, martyring himself to cycling's EPO era.

He subsequently testified in the US Anti-Doping Agency's (USADA) investigation into ​Armstrong, ‌who was stripped of his seven Tour titles in 2012 after the agency concluded he ran what it called the most sophisticated doping program the sport had seen.

Vaughters ⁠went on to found the team now ‌known as Cannondale-EF, positioning it as ‌an advocate for clean racing.

His remarks come as riders pushed back on the timing of ‌Sunday's tests.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Vingegaard, tested at 2 am, said the visit cost him about 40 minutes of sleep before a mountain stage on which he later ‌crashed ​and broke his collarbone.

Pogačar, ⁠tested at 5 am, said he managed only four hours of sleep. Stage winner Remco Evenepoel called the practice "inhuman".

The International Testing Agency, which oversees doping controls for the UCI, said it was "conscious that night-time testing may be disruptive ⁠to riders' rest and recovery" but that effective testing must, "in limited and justified circumstances," occur outside daytime hours.

Micro-dosing of substances ‌such as EPO ‌and human growth hormone, which can clear the body within ​hours, has long concerned anti-doping authorities, who say predictable daytime-only testing makes such practices easier to evade.

Reuters

title: Redevelopment of former hospital site to deliver 800 new homes
text:
A northern New South Wales mayor has welcomed the decision to increase the density of an large apartment complex at Tweed Heads, but has urged the state government to increase the amount of affordable housing in the complex.

The NSW government this week unveiled a master plan for the redevelopment the former Tweed Hospital site, creating 800 high-density units.

Meanwhile, housing density at the East Lismore housing development on land formerly owned by Southern Cross University has also increased from 400 to 500 homes.

The Minister for the North Coast, Janelle Saffin said Tweed Heads had a "dire need" for housing, with a vacancy rate of 0.8 per cent.

"Tweed Heads is one of the fastest-growing communities in NSW, which has placed immense pressure on local rental and housing markets," she said.

Tweed Mayor Chris Cherry welcomed the creation of more homes but said the state government could do better than allocating just 10 per cent of the development as affordable housing.

The former Tweed hospital site will be transformed into 800 new homes. (Supplied: NSW government)

"We certainly welcome this commitment by the state government to get housing onto that site. It's prime real estate in Tweed Heads, so to get a residential outcome is a great one," Cr Cherry said.

"It's ideal for affordable housing and getting some of that low-cost, key-worker housing that we really need in the shire.

"I'm hoping, because it's a public piece of land that the community already owns, that maybe we could get something a bit higher than that in terms of affordable housing," she said.

" Eighty units will help, but more is always better. "

The old hospital site is prime waterfront real estate. (ABC North Coast: Elloise Farrow-Smith)

Affordable housing 'negative' value

Landcom's executive general manager of the built form division, Andrew Cooper, said planning was still in the early stages, but confirmed at least 10 per cent of the development would be affordable housing.

Mr Cooper said there needed to be a mix of affordable and market value housing to make the development viable.

"Unfortunately, affordable housing has negative land value,. That's just the nature of the costs in 2026. You need the market housing to effectively support the affordable housing," he said.

Andrew Cooper says Landcom is committed to a component of affordable housing within their developments. (ABC North Coast: Cath Adams)

Teresa Mitchell, the founding director and CEO of AGAPE Outreach, was disappointed there was no social housing included in the development.

"The fact that there's no social housing is kind of sad. We've got sole parent pensioners, we've got people on disability payments, we've got youth, so not having any social housing is just not going to cater for any of these people.

"There are so many in this area in need of housing. We need housing, we really do. But, it's just not going to cater for the people who live here."

Homeless people living in tents in the banks of the Tweed River. (ABC North Coast: Elloise Farrow-Smith)

The State Significant Development Application for the Tweed Heads development will be lodged in early 2027 with construction expected to commence in 2029, pending planning approvals.

Meanwhile, the construction of 50 rent-to-buy units in East Lismore by Landcom was on-track for occupation by February, Mr Cooper said.

Next door, the state government has added an extra 100 homes to the 400-lot being developed by Landcom as part of the Reconstruction Authority's Resilient Lands Program.

Funding will also be provided to support infrastructure upgrades, such as water and sewerage, as required.

The first lots will be available for people relocating from homes bought back by the Reconstruction Authority as part of the Resilient Homes Program.

The retail sale of around 100 lots was expected to begin in October, and will be released over five years.

title: Ian doesn't want children. He's part of a growing trend among men
text:
Ian was 13 when his dad moved out. He says it was about then he first thought having kids wasn't for him.

"I was probably destined not to have them based on what I know about the males in my family," the 48-year-old from Adelaide/Tarntanya says.

"I didn't want to repeat my dad's mistakes."

Ian considers himself a "wanderer". He says his desire to "drift through the world a bit" means he didn't think fatherhood was an ideal fit.

About 15 per cent of Australian men say they don't want children, according to the 2025 Household, Income and Labour Dynamics in Australia (HILDA) survey.

That's up from 11 per cent in 2005.

Much of the research into the decision to be childfree, or voluntarily childless, has focused on women. Researchers say that is no surprise "since having children is more strongly socially prescribed for women" and women bear "greater costs and risks to having a child than men".

Imogene Smith, a clinical psychologist and lecture at The Cairnmillar Institute, is one of few Australian researchers who has studied child-free men.

She says talking about why men are turning way from fatherhood can help them feel supported in their decision.

Why more men are choosing not to have children

Family of origin was a common reason men shared for not wanting children in Dr Smith's surveys.

The difference between childfree and childless While not everyone in the childless or childfree communities will identify the same way, understanding the difference means we can respond more thoughtfully when learning about someone's parental status.

"[Fatherhood] didn't sound appealing to them," Dr Smith says.

Some touched on the relationship with their father "turning them away" from parenting, or having lots of siblings and seeing how hard it was for their parents, for example.

"It wasn't always a dad thing, but it was something to do with that template of what we all have in our mind of what it looks like to be a parent, which is firmly rooted in our parents," Dr Smith says.

She says for some, there was a desire to be "alternative".

"We had a fair bit of 'I'm different, I don't see myself in that way ... not wanting to follow the norm."

There were men who had felt firm in their decision from when they were young, says Dr Smith.

Participants also talked about various fears. For example, concerns about the climate or political environment, or passing on inheritable conditions around mental and physical health.

And despite Dr Smith's sample of men largely being middle class and professionals, a lot had financial concerns.

"They just couldn't see how they could afford a bigger house to have a kid," she says.

Are you a man who has chosen not to have children? We'd love to hear from you about why

Men were also influenced by the experience of raising children they saw around them, like their friends with kids, or watching parents at a cafe.

"A lot of uncles saying, 'Nope, I have seen my brother, and he has no free time'.

"One man said something about it ruining his sex life. He said, 'My friends who've got two kids, they have Dora the Explorer sex … however long that episode is, that's how long they've got to do it.'"

Do men face societal pressure to have kids?

While Ian knew in his teens that he didn't want kids, he says he gave himself permission to change his mind.

" I kept an open mind that whole time, if I changed my mind, I am allowed to change my mind. "

But that never happened. Ian met his wife, who "was of the same mindset" 16 years ago.

Their desire to stay childfree is part of what pulled them together, he says.

Ian says he's content with being a dog owner. (Supplied)

Child-free men have previously shared with ABC Lifestyle experiencing a pressure to carry on the family name, or judgement that they are not a "real man" if they don't have kids.

Dr Smith says most of the men she spoke with said any societal pressure they faced "just washes over" them.

"They just kind of laughed [when I asked about it] — they just weren't bothered."

Previous research has shown that childless men have poorer health outcomes, are at higher risk of social disconnection, and face negative stigma. But Dr Smith suspects that could be because much of the research focuses on older men of previous generations.

"If you waited until the current generation of 30-year-olds was 70 or 80, you might get a different result."

How child-free men are viewed by others

While Ian hasn't felt pressure to have children, he says his child-free status does raise eyebrows when meeting new people.

"Someone [at work] asked me just the other day 'Have you got kids? You don't ever do the school run?' I said 'I don't have kids' and they were shocked."

Tom Denson, a professor in the School of Psychology at UNSW, co-authored research published earlier this year looking at the negative bias child-free people experience.

It found child-free men experience less bias than child-free women for making the same choice not to have children.

"Child-free men were rated higher in interpersonal warmth than child-free women, who were viewed as less warm," Professor Denson says.

Sign up to the ABC Lifestyle newsletter Get a mid-week boost and receive easy recipes, wellbeing ideas, and home and garden tips in your inbox every Wednesday. You’ll also receive a monthly newsletter of our best recipes.

A study into the stigma and societal pressures for voluntary childless men and women found women are seen by society as "spinsters", while men were "living the dream".

Dr Smith says the men in her study "weren't overly concerned" about people judging them for their choice.

"They went to the effort of telling me, unprompted, what they do to contribute to society and leave their own kind of legacy.

"Some of them men were telling me about their charity work, places they donate to, some worked with young people.

"They felt quite OK about contributing to society in their own way. And had a strong sense of this choice doesn't make me less than somebody who has a child."

title: 'Don't go there': Pauline Hanson fires up in Q&A over White Australia policy
text:
One Nation leader Pauline Hanson has angrily denied suggestions her strident opposition to migration by anyone from "radical Islamic countries" amounts to a version of the White Australia policy.

Ms Hanson was speaking at a Perth leadership breakfast hosted by The West Australian newspaper after returning from the UK, where she had blamed the end of the White Australia policy for migration issues in Australia during an interview with far-right activist and convicted criminal Tommy Robinson.

During a question-and-answer session at this morning's event, Senator Hanson spoke about radicalisation and terrorism carried out by Muslims, namely last year's Bondi terror attack.

"You've got to call out the elephant in the room," she said.

"And I'm sorry to say this but I don't want people coming to this country from radical Islamic countries.

Pauline Hanson addresses the Leadership Matters event at Crown Perth. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

"I don't want that here. I want to protect the Australian people, I want them to feel safe in the streets. I don't want to see this antisemitism that's going on. I don't want to see the hatred that's happening.

"That's why I'm very strong on certain countries. You've got to say no."

'Nothing to do' with White Australia policy

Challenged by the event's host on whether this was a version of the White Australia policy, Senator Hanson strongly rejected the suggestion.

"No, it's not," she said.

"It's got nothing to do with that. It's got nothing to do with the White Australia policy at all. So don't go there with me, I tell you what, because I won't stand for it."

She said she had never supported the White Australia policy.

In a wide-ranging address to the event, Senator Hanson lauded Western Australia's small business community and said the state helped "pay this nation's bills", while generating a huge portion of business investment.

Pauline Hanson took part in a question and answer session after addressing the event. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

But she noted it also had an extremely low rental vacancy rate and again sought to blame migration.

Perth visit follows UK trip

Senator Hanson has just returned from a visit to Europe and the UK, where she spoke at a conservative conference and took part in the interview with Robinson.

Pauline Hanson pictured in London on the weekend. (ABC News: Riley Stuart)

Robinson quizzed Senator Hanson on how Australia had ended up with "Pakistanis, Somalis, all of these African problems with violent Africans?"

Senator Hanson said it "started" in 1973 when former prime minister Gough Whitlam "opened up and got rid of the White Australia policy".

The White Australia policy was the name given to a series of laws and administrative practices introduced after 1901 to restrict non-European immigration and preserve Australia as a predominantly white, British society.

Hanson singles out White Australia policy One Nation leader Pauline Hanson has blamed the end of the White Australia policy for migration issues in the country during a controversial podcast appearance with a far-right activist in the UK.

In her address to the business breakfast, Senator Hanson addressed the media attention she had received for meeting Robinson, saying: "I sat down with a lot of people, some I agree with, some I don't".

"But Australians are sick of politicians who only ever talk to people who already agree with them.

"I went to Britain to see up close what happens when a government gets things wrong. Immigration. Spending. Energy."

Senator Hanson was asked by the event host who would be paying for her trip to Europe, where she was pictured at a luxury villa in Sicily with mining magnate Gina Rinehart and attended a fashion party.

"It didn't cost the taxpayer one cent for my trip overseas. Not one cent," she said.

Hanson risks losing everyday Australians while busy with the richest Many voters are unhappy with the hand they feel successive governments have dealt them and no-one has tapped into that better than Pauline Hanson.

She was not pressed further on the issue but said that no one's private life was questioned like hers.

With One Nation polling strongly, Senator Hanson said she "had no doubt" she could do the job of prime minister.

"It's not about Pauline Hanson becoming prime minister. I want the right person in the right job. And if someone I believe can do a better job than I can, then go for it."

Quizzed on whether high-profile One Nation MP Barnaby Joyce could be that person, she said she had not discussed that with him but Mr Joyce, a former deputy prime minister, had the experience.

One Nation eyes WA by-election

During a media conference after the event, Senator Hanson said no deal had been struck with the WA Liberals on preferences for an upcoming state by-election in Perth's far southern suburbs.

Pauline Hanson speaks at a press conference following her appearance at a leadership breakfast event in Perth. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

But WA Opposition Leader Basil Zempilas has said he was open to finding a way to work with One Nation, and Ms Hanson told reporters she would "definitely" like to work with Mr Zempilas.

Meet the candidates in the Secret Harbour by-election Here's everything you need to know about the Secret Harbour by-election, which is shaping up as one of the most closely-watched political contests in WA for some time.

One Nation's candidate in the poll, Luke Herdegen, was asked about a 2019 social media post where he admitted heavily using cocaine and how he had changed his life since.

"That's a story I put out there to try and help inspire people to live happier and healthier lives and I think it really did help a lot of people," Mr Herdegen said.

"When I'm in the streets of Secret Harbour they're not asking me about a blog post from 10 years ago."

Luke Herdegen answers questions from media on Tuesday. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

On the weekend, One Nation state MP Rod Caddies was vaunting the thoroughness of the party's candidate vetting process.

But when Senator Hanson was questioned about Mr Herdegen's cocaine use earlier in the media conference, she said she did not know anything about it.

"I have been out of the country and I just briefly met Luke yesterday," she said.

Protesters rally outside

Around 80 protesters gathered outside Crown Perth ahead of the event.

Around 80 protesters gathered outside Crown Perth on Tuesday. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

The protest, organised by the WA Socialists and the WA Greens, was positioned at the entrance to the venue with protesters chanting at cars as they entered.

Protesters held signs showing Ms Hanson's face and chanted phrases such as "migrants are welcome, racists are not" and "Seven West you can't hide", referring to The West Australian's owner.

Mining magnate Gina Rinehart, a powerful supporter of Hanson, has a 9.15 per cent interest in Seven West's parent company Southern Cross Media via an investment made by her Hancock Prospecting company.

There was a heavy police presence at the protest with around 17 officers positioned around the group, as well as police horses.

Around 80 protesters gathered outside Crown Perth ahead of the event. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

Senator Hanson yesterday visited the electorate of Secret Harbour, south of Perth, where a state by-election will be held on August 29.

She was seen speaking to people in Secret Harbour alongside Mr Herdegen, a gym owner and former champion weightlifter.

It is the second time Senator Hanson has travelled to Western Australia in as many months.

title: Weatherald retains Australian Test spot despite middling Ashes returns
text:
Opening batsman Jake Weatherald has retained his spot in Australia's Test squad for the looming series against Bangladesh.

Despite a middling Ashes campaign last summer, Weatherald is among a 13-man squad bolstered by the return of a trio of big guns.

Captain Pat Cummins, paceman Josh Hazlewood and spinner Nathan Lyon all return after recovering from injury.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

"They have worked extremely hard, individually and with the [fitness] teams, over the past few months to return from their respective injuries," Australia's chairman of selectors George Bailey said.

"While this is a 13-player squad, we have players prepared and available should any adjustments be required in the lead-up to these matches.

"The volume of cricket and schedule across the next 12 months are likely to present further opportunities across all formats for many players."

Missing from Australia's squad for the final Test against England last January are Usman Khawaja (retired), Michael Neser, Todd Murphy and Brendan Doggett.

The Australians host Bangladesh in Darwin from August 13-17 and Mackay from August 22-26, the start of a gruelling 12 months featuring 20 Test matches.

Weatherald will resume his opening batting partnership with Travis Head after playing all five Tests last summer against England.

The Darwin-born left-hander made only 201 runs at an average of 22.33 but has been given another chance to enhance his Test credentials.

AAP

title: Job system broken for workers, booming for private equity, report says
text:
Australia's system of privatised employment services is structurally broken and its prized federal contracts are being used to enrich millionaires and private equity firms, a new report argues.

An investigation by the Centre for International Corporate Tax Accountability and Research (CICTAR) has found an "alarming lack of transparency" from major government contractors operating inside the privatised employment services system.

It has also raised concerns about the potential for conflicts of interest arising from for-profit companies in the system making large donations to Australia's major political parties.

Employment services to get largest overhaul in 30 years Under the proposal, unemployed Australians seeking assistance would be placed into three tailored support tiers

The employment services industry is supposed to help unemployed Australians find work by providing them with tailored job search support.

Employment services are the federal government's single largest procurement outside Defence, with contracts worth $5.5 billion underpinning the system's for-profit and not-for-profit contractors, provided under the banner of Workforce Australia Services.

Participation in Workforce Australia Services is compulsory for many people who receive Centrelink's JobSeeker payments.

But the CICTAR report argues that the system has become a lucrative vehicle for private equity-controlled multinationals and wealthy executives to extract massive profits from taxpayer funds, with some providers developing a near-total dependence on lucrative government contracts.

It shows how for-profit "employment services providers" can use aggressive tax minimisation, large dividend payouts, offshore-related party payments, tax havens, and opaque financial reporting to extract profits from the system, without breaking any laws.

And it questions if the system is meeting the public's expectations.

"It's no secret that Australia's system of privatised employment services has failed," the report argues.

"The Howard-era system fills the pockets of private equity and millionaires, while leaving jobseekers without work."

The CICTAR report

The report, 'Who's rorting who? The failure of outsourced job services', was released today.

It says AusTender data shows employment services contracts currently total $5.5 billion, shared between 45 providers.

Changes to employment services The government isn't rebuilding the Commonwealth Employment Service, but will its proposed changes fix a broken system?

The Department of Employment and Workplace Relations (DEWR) grants the licences to deliver generalist or specialist employment services (such as to Indigenous Australians, refugees, and ex-offenders). Each licence is designated for a geographic region in Australia.

Just nine for-profit providers — APM, atWork Australia, MAX, Asuria, Teldraw, Sureway, Sarina Russo, RNTT and Jobfind Centres — hold more than a third of the total employment services contract value, at $2.1 billion. A single provider, APM, holds 23 licences and a contract worth $590.7 million.

Many of those providers are recipients of government contracts in other areas, such as aged and disability care.

As the report shows, private-equity-controlled for-profit companies play a significant role in Australia's system (the two largest for-profit providers, APM and atWork, are both controlled by private equity). Top employment services providers have become notable as sources of wealth for high-profile Australians.

The report looks in greater detail at four of Australia's for-profit employment services providers — APM, atWork, Sarina Russo Job Access and Jobs Statewide — which hold contracts worth a collective total of $1.24 billion.

It uses those four case studies to show how difficult it is to analyse the complicated corporate ownership structure of private-equity-controlled multinationals and for-profit businesses to find out what types of tax arrangements they are using and how much tax they are paying, to see if Australians are getting value for money.

"All four corporations are heavily dependent on government contracts," the report notes.

"When individual or corporate empires are built on the back of government funding to support the unemployed, it is appropriate to ask: is this an efficient and effective use of government resources?"

In response to questions from CICTAR, both APM and the operator of Jobs Statewide said their programs were long-standing and successful in helping people into work.

The 'biggest shake-up in 30 years'?

In 2023, a Labor-dominated parliamentary committee reviewed Australia's multi-decade experiment with privatisation and an outsourced quasi-market system for employment services, and it called for the system to be completely rebuilt.

It found that after decades of outsourcing, the Australian public service now had little corporate memory or experience of the complexities of employment service delivery, so it could not even judge if the billion-dollar contracts it was awarding to private sector job agencies were buying value for money.

"We have an inefficient, outsourced, fragmented social security compliance management system that sometimes gets someone a job against all odds," it said.

Review recommends ground-up rebuild of employment services A major inquiry established in the weeks after the government won the 2022 federal election has handed down recommendations that would completely rebuild Australia's employment services system.

"Every country that has experimented with extensive outsourcing has eventually realised that it does not work without a public sector core, as some companies will profiteer."

In May this year, the Albanese government announced the biggest shake-up to Australia's employment services system in 30 years.

At the time, Amanda Rishworth, the federal employment minister, said she wanted to build a "new employment service" to fix the problems that had plagued the industry for decades.

However, she said contracted for-profit service providers would still play a major role in her new system.

The CICTAR report challenges the idea that for-profit service providers should be part of the new system.

CICTAR is funded by grants from trusts and foundations, and by trade unions from overseas and in Australia, including the Community and Public Sector Union (CPSU) and the New South Wales Nurses and Midwifery Association (NSWNMF).

Recommendations for a new public model

The CICTAR report makes four recommendations.

First, it says employment services should be entirely returned to public sector delivery for both online and in-person services because the privatised model has failed in Australia.

Migrant services groups react to Workforce Australia changes Personalised support helped domestic violence survivor and single mother Harsimran get a job, despite significant barriers. It's an approach that could benefit more jobseekers following the federal government's revamp of Workforce Australia.

It argues that the public sector would lower costs by removing the profit motive from employment service delivery, eliminate offshore profit-shifting and dividend payouts to shareholders, and provide higher-quality and more accountable services for jobseekers and employers.

It says non-profit organisations could still deliver specialist services in the new system.

Second, it says the procurement of services must be strengthened in the meantime. It says the government must enforce stricter transparency standards for contractors in the current system.

It says that should include publishing the full text of all service delivery contracts, banning entities that use aggressive tax minimisation or fail to file timely financial statements, requiring full Tier 1 financial disclosures for any entity receiving over $10 million in funding, and subjecting private equity acquisitions of government-funded firms to intense scrutiny regarding their corporate structures and ultimate owners.

Third, it says providers need to be properly scrutinised across all funding streams.

It says since many employment services providers operate across multiple government sectors — such as aged care, disability, and childcare — Australia's procurement rules must be unified so that if a provider is screened out or disqualified from one government service area then it should be automatically barred from receiving contracts in other areas.

Fourth, it says political donations by government contractors are a risk to the integrity, or perceived integrity, of Australia's political system and the practice should be prohibited.

"Entities that hold government contracts above a certain threshold, together with their shareholders and key management personnel, should be prohibited from making political donations," it says.

"This would be most appropriately achieved by reforming the Commonwealth Electoral Act."

title: Buckley opts out of Dons, Blues coaching races after Tasmania snub
text:
A disappointed Nathan Buckley is ruling out bidding for other vacant AFL head coaching jobs after being overlooked by the Tasmania Devils.

While Buckley, who openly coveted the Tasmanian post that went to Ken Hinkley, says he wants to be a head coach again, he is unlikely to pitch for the vacant positions at Essendon and Carlton.

"I haven't really reflected on where those roles sit, and I don't think I would enter those processes," Buckley told Fox Footy on Monday night.

Loading...

Buckley said his most likely outcome next year was to remain at Geelong, where he is an assistant to Chris Scott.

"I am absolutely focused on the next six to 10 weeks with Geelong and how that plays out," he said.

"I will take some time to reassess what is after that."

Buckley was committed to winning the Devils job that instead was given to former Port Adelaide boss Hinkley.

"I went home, kicked the dog, threw the toys out of the cot … because I was really keen on it," he said.

"If you're going to go for a job, you have got to decide if you're absolutely all in on it — and I was.

"And I absolutely respect the process that the Devils have run, and I respect their decision with Ken as well. I am glad they have found their man, and they're really clear that Kenny is the guy for them."

However, the former Collingwood boss said the Devils' decision was a personal "fork in the road".

"I was really deliberate in getting back into Geelong, and that was as much to give myself the very best chance to be ready to go for the Tasmania role," he said.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

"Now that is off the table, so I will be absolutely committed to what is going on at Geelong. I have got an option for next year there and that is probably the most likely outcome.

"There are no guarantees in this, and you have got to put yourself out there, and you have got to have a crack.

"So there's disappointment."

Asked if he remained steadfast in wanting to be a head coach again, Buckley replied: "Yeah. I determined that about 12 months ago.

"That is what I believe I am capable of doing. It's what I want to do."

AAP

title: SA government to explore legal options against developer
text:
South Australia's cabinet has authorised the SA Commissioner for Business Services to undertake "active examination of all of the legal options" to hold developers Wel.Co "to account".

The national company axed 75 contracts with homebuyers for stages eight and nine of its Springwood Estate at Gawler East last week.

It said changes to the planning of the broader area, including roads and infrastructure, meant the affected stages "could no longer be delivered as purchasers had originally contracted".

"What was originally expected to operate as a local road is now planned to become a major distributor road carrying substantially more traffic," Wel.Co said in a statement.

"That fundamentally changes the living environment for the adjoining homes [in the development].

"This issue is isolated to Stages 8 and 9 of Springwood [and] Wel.Co's broader project portfolio continues to progress as planned."

Nick Champion says a larger roundabout and a larger road will be added to the Springwood stage eight and nine development area. (ABC News: Carl Saville)

SA Planning Minister Nick Champion told 891 ABC Adelaide's Rory McClaren on Friday the contracts were cancelled without "real justification".

"Nothing the state government's done in the provision of infrastructure in any way would lead to this decision by this company," Mr Champion said.

'Deserve to be held to account'

Premier Peter Malinauskas said the situation had demonstrated clauses that allow developers to walk away from contracts should be closely examined.

"Where actors seek to tear up contracts for purposes that aren't consistent with what they say, then they deserve to be held to account," he said.

The premier said the government would assess its legislative options to "regulate this type of practice" alongside exploring the available legal options to respond.

"Unless the contracts are reinstated … I've made it crystal clear that I expect the commissioner … to examine all our options here," Mr Malinauskas said.

"What we won't abide by is people participating in this particular industry and doing over hardworking mums and dads in the process.

"It doesn't accord with basic decency."

In a statement, Wel.Co said it was continuing discussions with the Town of Gawler Council "regarding planning matters" for stage eight and nine of Springwood, but said it would be inappropriate to comment further at this time.

Concern for other developments

More than 200 kilometres away in the Riverland town of Renmark, Wel.Co also holds a contract with the Renmark Paringa Council to develop about 800 new homes.

Loading Vimeo content

It is an extension to the existing Jane Eliza River Estate and is also expected to deliver an expanded marina and marine industry precinct.

Following the issues at Springwood, some locals are concerned for the stability of the Jane Eliza Development.

Renmark resident Craig Leuders has opposed the development since it was proposed about a decade ago because of concerns about the site and said he was concerned the developers would pull out after a certain stage.

"I think the same thing is going to happen here," he said.

"I'm not against development, Renmark's ready to grow, but not at the cost of the other people that are already here."

Craig Leuders has opposed the Jane Eliza extension since it was proposed. (ABC Riverland: Anita Ward)

Wel.Co said Jane Eliza was separate to Springwood and in a different planning jurisdiction.

"It would not be appropriate to draw comparisons with Springwood," the company said.

"The project continues to progress through the planning process, and Wel.Co remains excited to bring Jane Eliza to the Riverland."

Renmark Paringa Council began working with Wel.Co on the Jane Eliza development in July 2022 and entered into a Land Facilitation Agreement in March 2025.

The Renmark Paringa Council is working with developer Wel.Co on the Jane Eliza extension in SA's Riverland region. (Supplied: Renmark Paringa Council)

The council commissioned hydrology modelling on the development area in 2025, which found the extension would not significantly impact the flood plain or the wider community in the event of future flooding.

"At this stage, council has no reason to suggest the issues reported in relation to the Springwood development have any impact on the Jane Eliza project," Renmark Paringa Council chief executive Tony Siviour said in a statement.

"Jane Eliza remains a fundamental project for the Riverland, delivering much needed housing opportunities to support our growing community and workforce.

"Council remains committed to working collaboratively with Wel.Co to achieve the successful delivery of this development."

title: Live: Holocaust survivors have PTSD from antisemitic events, commission hears
text:
Mr Sztrajt says the school has increasingly been managing antisemitic incidents over time.

"Unfortunately, by being probably the most prominent Jewish school or the most well-known Jewish school in the state, that comes with it … being a target," he says.

He details an incident in May 2024, where the words "Jew die" had been graffitied at the school. A social media video of the graffiti went viral, triggering serious concern from families.

"There were families who had told me personally that they would not be sending their children to school," he says.

He says the school chose to hang up an Australian flag and a flag with Hebrew words for "The Jewish people live" in response.

title: Millions greet Spain's WC heroes as FIFA probes ugly post-match fights
text:
Spain's World Cup-winning team has been met with an estimated two million fans at a raucous championship parade through the streets of Madrid.

The 26 players and their coaching staff met with the country's royals, and then with Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez, after arriving in the Spanish capital a day after beating Argentina 1-0 in the final in New Jersey.

"Thank you to the coaches and players for the style of play, the effort and the victory," Sánchez said. "We have truly enjoyed watching you play."

Spain's World Cup win a reminder collective spirit trumps solo stars As some of football's biggest names lit up the World Cup, Spain flew under the radar all the way to hoisting the trophy on its final day.

The squad then boarded an open-top bus for a raucous parade through central Madrid. "Stars shine together" was written on the bus, which also carried photos of the footballers.

Players — carrying the World Cup trophy and wearing shirts with the words Somos Campeones (We Are Champions) — danced and interacted with the fans along the route.

"It's been an emotional and proud experience to represent a wonderful country with such passionate and devoted fans," Spain coach Luis de la Fuente said before the parade started. "We are filled with joy."

The squad travelled down Madrid's historic avenues from near Moncloa Palace, the prime minister's official residence, to Cibeles Square, where authorities told local media that some 120,000 people were on hand for a ceremony.

Players were introduced one by one before entering the stage set up at the square. Each picked the song to be played when they came in. Álex Baena, celebrating his 25th birthday, entered with the World Cup trophy.

Coach De la Fuente was thrust into the air by his players after being introduced. The coach was among those who sang along to the song being played for them.

Striker Borja Iglesias featured as a DJ at one point, with his teammates dancing on the stage.

Singers Aitana, Ana Mena and Lola Índigo were among those entertaining the crowd and the players.

Thousands of Spanish fans gathered around the Cibeles Fountain (Fuente de Cibeles) in Madrid to celebrate their World Cup win. (Ruben Albarran/Anadolu via Getty Images)

The ceremony ended with the whole Spain squad lifting the trophy again, just like at the awards ceremony on Sunday, but now in front of the local crowd.

Groups of fans had begun securing their spots near Cibeles late Monday morning local time ahead of the team's arrival, bringing umbrellas to protect against the sun and temperatures of up to 35 degrees Celsius.

The square is where previous Spain teams and their supporters have typically gathered to celebrate. It is also the traditional celebration spot for Real Madrid when it wins titles.

FIFA launches investigation into ugly post-game fracas

Argentina's Leandro Paredes was given a red card after fighting Spain's Gavi following the final whistle. (Getty Images: Marvin Ibo Guengoer - GES Sportfoto/Getty Images)

FIFA opened an investigation Monday after Spain's celebrations on beating Argentina in the World Cup final took an ugly turn when players briefly clashed on the field.

Video replays showed several players involved in a scuffle, including Argentina midfielder Leandro Paredes.

He appeared to shove Spain's Eric Garcia by the throat. When Spain substitute Gavi jumped in to intervene, Paredes pushed him to the ground.

Spain captain Rodri also seemed to be hit in the stomach by Argentina defender Nahuel Molina as he ran past to join teammates celebrating the title win.

Molina later crowded Rodri, who was the target of a verbal confrontation by his former Manchester City teammate Nicolas Otamendi.

Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni and other players rushed in to de-escalate the situation.

FIFA said in a statement its "disciplinary committee has appointed a Disciplinary and Ethics Prosecutor to investigate potential breaches of the [FIFA disciplinary code] with regard to the post-match incidents".

The brief statement gave no timetable for an investigation and did not name Paredes, who was not shown a red card after the game yet could face a suspension.

Spain needed extra time to win Sunday's final 1-0 after Argentina was reduced to 10 men. Enzo Fernandez was sent off with a second yellow card in stoppage time at the end of 90 minutes.

The Argentina players stood facing their fans, with their backs to the podium, when Spain's squad lifted the trophy. It was an unusual show of disrespect to the new champions, who had given their opponents a guard of honour to collect their medals.

AP

title: Union boss accused of 'bullying' by Fire Rescue Victoria commissioner
text:
Victoria's fire rescue commissioner Gavin Freeman has accused the firefighters' union head of bullying and intimidating Fire Rescue Victoria (FRV) staff and himself.

In a letter to United Firefighters Union (UFU) secretary Peter Marshall, dated yesterday, the commissioner wrote to express concern about "excessively aggressive and inappropriately personal communications" disseminated on its website and social media and in media statements.

"These communications form part of an ongoing campaign of harassment and intimidation directed at sections of FRV's workforce — including its own members when they take action in support of the organisation's interests, rather than the union's preferred position," Mr Freeman wrote.

Gavin Freeman says the United Firefighters Union has targeted him with "hurtful and derogatory content". (ABC News)

But Mr Marshall told the ABC he rejected the letter "in its entirety" and said his union's scrutiny of Mr Freeman and other FRV officials was legitimate.

Mr Marshall said the heavily unionised FRV workforce last year passed a majority vote of no confidence in Commissioner Freeman.

"I welcome fair and legitimate scrutiny of my performance, as does my leadership team," Mr Freeman said in a statement to the ABC today.

"However, scrutiny does not extend to harassment, bullying and intimidation," he said.

"Our people deserve to feel safe and be treated with dignity, professionalism and respect."

Commissioner raises psychological safety concerns

In the letter to Mr Marshall, which was shared with thousands of FRV staff, Mr Freeman wrote he'd received "multiple reports from FRV employees" who were concerned about the UFU's portrayal of themselves.

He said this had contributed to a workplace environment where employees felt "targeted, distressed and reluctant to participate in legitimate organisational processes for fear of public vilification or reputational harm".

A graphic on the UFU website indicating the results of a confidence vote in FRV leadership. (Supplied)

Mr Freeman said he'd already written to the union "countless times" about the issue.

He said FRV had a duty to take reasonable steps to protect its employees' psychological safety, including taking legal action and reporting matters "to the appropriate authorities".

Mr Freeman, who has been commissioner since 2023, also claimed FRV executives Tony Matthews, Tom McPherson and himself had been targeted by "hurtful and derogatory content" in a no-confidence campaign for more than a year.

This included the distribution of a satirical colouring-in sheet depicting the images of the three of them to children at a recruit graduation ceremony, he wrote.

Mr Freeman said FRV had received reports from its own staff of many of them feeling "psychologically unsafe" when exposed to such content at work.

"As you're aware, the UFU holds a unique position in terms of the level of access it has to FRV's workplaces, employees and systems," Mr Freeman wrote.

" This position must not be abused. "

Union says its criticisms are justified

A statement from the UFU said given the FRV commissioner was a public official, scrutiny of his performance "by his workforce, by their union, and by the public — is legitimate, it is protected, and it will continue".

Mr Marshall said his members' grievances with FRV officials included an ongoing dispute over an enterprise bargaining agreement and removal of income protection for firefighters.

He said ongoing problems with inadequate and out-of-date firefighting equipment were also a source of major frustration, which the union raised in the wake of a fatal house fire in Werribee last month.

The union used messaging on fire trucks during a protracted industrial dispute with FRV and the government. (Supplied)

The union repeated its call for Mr Freeman to resign and said it would not tone down its message.

"The UFU will not be intimidated, and we will not stop advocating for our members and for the safety of the Victorian community," Mr Marshall said.

" If the commissioner wishes to restore confidence, the path is clear: fix the failures, settle the agreement, and lead. "

The letter from Mr Freeman is the latest incident in a series of long-running and bitter disputes between FRV and the union.

FRV last year sued the union for deceptive and misleading conduct over an income protection scheme.

At the time, Mr Freeman said he had concerns about the "integrity" of the income protection scheme that has been running since 2023.

The UFU earlier this month lost a Supreme Court bid to keep secret a corruption probe into its dealings with the government of former Victorian premier Daniel Andrews.

Mr Marshall and the union had sought to block the release of the Independent Broad-based Anti-corruption Commission's Operation Richmond report.

title: Aussie world champs name new rugby sevens coach for LA Olympics
text:
Reg Tayler has been named head coach of the world champion Australian women's rugby sevens team, as the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics loom on the horizon.

The decision completes a succession plan following Tim Walsh's departure from the position after Australia clinched the World Championship in France last month.

Tayler was an assistant coach to Walsh, who has since become Rugby Australia's (RA) director of women's high performance.

New Australia coach Reg Tayler (right) pictured with Tim Walsh. (Rugby Australia)

Tayler will guide Australia through its two-year build-up to the Olympics.

"The squad is determined for continued success through to the Olympics in Los Angeles," Tayler said in an RA statement.

Rugby Australia setting high standards Only three years out from hosting the Women's Rugby World Cup, Australia makes a key appointment it hopes will help deliver success on the international stage.

"Our players are professional and dedicated to achieving a gold medal, and I am confident we will have the squad capable of that in 2028."

Tayler, who joined the Australian sevens program last year, has big shoes to fill.

Walsh not only led Australia to its recent World Championship victory but was also in charge when it won the inaugural Olympic gold in women's rugby sevens at the 2016 Rio de Janeiro Games.

It is the only time Australia has won Olympic gold in the sport.

Australia won the first women's Olympic rugby sevens gold on offer in 2016. (Getty Images/World Rugby: Dan Mullan)

Walsh, who coached Australia across two stints, will still have input through his newly created role as part of RA's strategy to align the sevens and Wallaroos programs.

He is confident Tayler will leave his mark as head coach, describing the appointment as a "seamless acquisition" for the squad.

"Reg is highly knowledgeable of the World Series circuit and has worked with many of our players coming through the pathways system," Walsh said.

"He's not going in cold, he completely understands the game and his expertise is going to be fully utilised by the program.

"It's a privilege and honour to retain Reg."

Tayler has previously coached the Australia A women's sevens team.

He also spent time working with Ireland's women's and men's squads between 2023-25.

Australia claimed the World Championship with victories in the final two legs of the series in Valladolid and Bordeaux.

title: PMOS/PCOS linked to 'significantly higher' stroke, heart attack risk
text:
Women with polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome (PMOS) have a "significantly higher" risk of cardiovascular disease than women without the condition, according to a new study.

The study, published today in The Lancet Obstetrics, Gynaecology, & Women's Health, analysed health insurance data collected from more than 2.4 million US women between 2000 and 2022.

It found women with PMOS (previously known as polycystic ovary syndrome or PCOS) were four times more likely to have cardiovascular events such as a heart attack or stroke than those without.

The paper's lead authors — reproductive endocrinologist Snigdha Alur-Gupta of the University of Rochester and Anuja Dokras, a professor of obstetrics and gynaecology at the University of Pennsylvania — said this was the largest study to explore the relationship between PMOS and cardiovascular risk to date.

Healthy eating and regular exercise may reduce the long-term heart disease risk for people with PMOS. (Unsplash: Jonathan Borba)

Dr Alur-Gupta and Professor Dokras said prior to the study, it was established that women with PMOS had an "increased risk of multiple cardiovascular disease risk factors" — such as type 2 diabetes.

But, they said, it was "notable" that even after taking into account obesity and other cardiovascular disease risk factors, or looking at data from younger women, a "higher hazard" remained for people with PMOS.

Dr Alur-Gupta and Professor Dokras said the research emphasised the importance of early heart health screening for people with PMOS, as well as other preventative measures, such as healthy eating and regular exercise.

"Our findings highlight the need for healthcare provider and patient education and awareness to improve early screening, counselling and long-term surveillance in this population," they said.

PMOS and cardiovascular health

PMOS, a common hormonal and metabolic disorder that affects 170 million women worldwide, can lead to infertility, metabolic conditions such as type 2 diabetes and mental health issues.

The condition was renamed from PCOS in May in a move that emphasised its hormonal and metabolic impacts, according to Helena Teede of the Monash Centre for Health Research Implementation.

The name of this health condition was 'very inaccurate'. Now it's changed Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) has been renamed polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome (PMOS), a move health experts say will lead to faster diagnoses and better treatment for women of all ages.

Professor Teede, who led the renaming initiative, did not contribute to the new study and said its link between PMOS and cardiovascular disease was significant, but not surprising.

She said women with PMOS experienced a disturbance of their endocrine system, which meant chemical messengers (hormones) were disrupted as they tried to communicate with other parts of the body.

"That disruption affects a whole body with many different manifestations," Professor Teede said.

"Hormone levels like insulin levels are associated with diabetes and heart disease, and they're all increased in this condition."

Helena Teede has led several studies on PMOS and spearheaded the condition's recent name change from PCOS. (Supplied: Monash University)

Heart Foundation senior manager for healthcare programs and clinical strategy Natalie Raffoul, who is a registered pharmacist and did not contribute to the study, said there hadn't been many high-quality studies probing the link between PMOS and cardiovascular health to date.

"Studies like this help us to recognise the lifelong care that is required after a diagnosis of PMOS, including regular screening for things like diabetes, which are well-known to be associated with the condition as well," she said.

Does the same risk exist in Australia?

In their study, US researchers used medical claims data from 413,450 pre-menopausal women with PMOS between the age of 18 and 50, comparing the data to more than 2 million women without the condition.

Dr Alur-Gupta and Professor Dokras said after factoring in risk factors — such as high blood pressure, high cholesterol and diabetes — cardiovascular disease risk only accounted for about a third of the relationship between PMOS and arteriosclerotic cardiovascular diseases that involve thickening of the arteries such as heart attacks and strokes.

The study was observational, which meant that although it identified a link between PMOS and cardiovascular disease, it didn't prove the condition caused it.

Women with PMOS have a higher prevalence of central obesity and insulin resistance, which is associated with an increased long-term risk of high blood pressure. (Unsplash: CDC)

While the study was conducted in the US, which has different demographics to Australia, Professor Teede said she expected the results would be similar here.

Dr Alur-Gupta and Professor Dokras agreed.

"As we've shown that the higher hazard exists even outside of these traditional risk factors, [we] do think that a higher hazard would be expected even in women with PMOS in Australia," they said.

Heart health screening needed

Dr Alur-Gupta and Professor Dokras said more research was needed to understand the potential biological factors underpinning the condition which could impact cardiovascular health.

"We showed through our analyses that the higher hazards are not solely attributable to cardiovascular risk factors," they said.

"Therefore, this association should be discussed even in patients without traditional risk factors such as hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and obesity."

ABC Health in your Instagram feed Follow @abchealth on Instagram, where we're busting myths and sharing practical, smart health advice.

Professor Teede and Ms Raffoul both welcomed the idea of increased screening and education for women with PMOS.

Professor Teede said her team recently analysed global heart disease guidelines and found "virtually none" mentioned PMOS as a potential risk factor.

"We should be aware these women are at high risk," she said.

"We should make sure we're focusing on prevention [of cardiovascular disease]."

title: NSW gold-antimony plant springs to life as Larvotto nears production
text:
Advertisement

Sponsored BusinessCompaniesBulls N' Bears NSW gold-antimony plant springs to life as Larvotto nears production Brought to you by BULLS N’ BEARS Doug Bright July 21, 2026 — 12:58pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

In a red-letter milestone for the company, Larvotto Resources has kicked off staged commissioning at its Hillgrove antimony-gold mine in New South Wales, marking the next step in a carefully sequenced restart that began underground at its nearby Metz mine. Larvotto’s underground contractor PYBAR has been pushing forward with the Metz workings and rehabilitating old drives since mining restarted, with first development ore delivered to surface stockpiles in May and ore stockpiles continuing to build since then. Larvotto Resources’ secondary crusher with the fine ore storage behind, at the company’s Hillgrove antimony-gold operation in NSW. That ore has now begun moving from stockpiles to the run-of-mine pad, before being fed into the primary jaw crusher, closed-circuit secondary crusher and screening circuit. From there, the material is then conveyed to the fine ore bin. The company says the crushing, screening and fine ore storage section has been successfully commissioned as the first stage of the plant restart. The resulting ore is now sitting in storage ready to feed the milling circuit during the upcoming phase of wet-end commissioning.

Advertisement

‘Hillgrove will become Australia’s largest antimony producer, and with tungsten potential growing.’ Larvotto Resources managing director Ron Heeks Once the fine ore bin is full, Larvotto plans to run the crushing circuit on waste for a limited period to generate crushed rock for various engineering requirements, allowing more time to bed the crushing system down. It will also give operators familiarisation time on the circuit. EPCM contractor Interquip, meanwhile, is completing its work on the communications, electrical, instrumentation and control systems at the wet end of the plant before that phase is commissioned. Hillgrove is forecast to become Australia’s second and largest producing antimony mine, with Larvotto pointing to the planned production of around 4900 tonnes of antimony -almost seven per cent of global production - and more than 40,000 ounces of gold a year across the current seven-year mine life. The company says its tungsten testwork is continuing, with multiple zones of tungsten mineralisation identified and unrecovered tungsten from previous operations sitting in the old tailings dam, all pointing to the potential for a third critical minerals string to Hillgrove’s bow.

Advertisement

Larvotto Resources managing director Ron Heeks said: “The commencement of commissioning is a defining milestone for Larvotto and reflects the significant progress made over the past twelve months since the plant refurbishment started. To reach this point is a tremendous achievement by our operations team and Interquip, the build contractors, and demonstrates the quality of execution, especially given the unique challenges that were, and continue to be, created by the Hormuz conflict on equipment deliveries.” Larvotto’s marketing pathways are also falling into place. Last month, the company locked in a binding seven-year offtake deal with Glencore International for its Hillgrove gold concentrate, complementing its previously announced antimony concentrate agreement with Wogen Resources. The Glencore agreement is expected to cover 15,000 dry metric tonnes of gold concentrate a year. Larvotto is also pushing ahead with its Modification 5 application process with the NSW government, seeking approval to lift its processing capacity from 250,000 to 500,000 tonnes a year, while also enabling dry-stack tailings and other environmental improvements. The company says responses to questions from the NSW Department of Planning, Housing and Infrastructure and other stakeholders are being finalised and are expected to be submitted by the end of July. Larvotto does not view the matters raised as material issues, instead describing them as being part of the appropriate level of review expected for a project of Hillgrove’s scale and strategic importance. With ore already passing through the front end of the plant, offtake partners signed up for its key concentrate products and the early stages of its process plant commissioning underway, Hillgrove is steadily working through its transition from development to a producing mine.

Advertisement

For Larvotto, the next few weeks will be all about transforming a historic mining field into a modern critical minerals operation, with antimony and gold leading the production charge and the prospect of a valuable tungsten by-product adding another layer of upside. NationalWAOne Nation No preference deal yet, but Hanson vows to put Labor last in Secret Harbour byelection Michael Philipps July 21, 2026 — 12:57pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

One Nation leader Pauline Hanson says a preference deal is yet to be struck with WA’s Liberal party ahead of the upcoming Secret Harbour byelection, but vowed to put both the Greens and Labor last. Speaking after her appearance at a business breakfast hosted by The West Australian, the federal senator said she hoped to work with the Liberals and Nationals to take the seat from Labor. One Nation leader Pauline Hanson campaigning with candidate Luke Herdegen in Secret Harbour on Monday. “This will be a test case, the same as when we won seven seats in the South Australian parliament,” Hanson said. “A deal has not been struck, but we will be putting Labor last and the Greens second last.”

Advertisement

The Queensland senator also dubbed Gina Rinehart “a wonderful West Australian” while defending the billionaire mining heiress’ support for One Nation. Hanson has received criticism from opposing political parties and the media after Rinehart provided significant support for the Queenslander, including an airplane, and as photos emerged of the pair holidaying in Europe. “Look at those taxes, royalties, and jobs that she provides the state,” Hanson told the audience after the Hancock Prospecting boss was brought up by West Australian editor Chris Dore at the breakfast event. “It didn’t cost the taxpayer one cent for my trip overseas. Not one cent.

Advertisement

“You think that she’s come with a silver spoon. She didn’t. “She had to borrow everything and was flat out in the very start to pay the bills. She’s built up that company. “A very smart, clever woman. And even now, to this day, who supports the [Royal] Flying Doctor Service. Who supports the Olympic swimmers and the lobbyists. “She’s a very proud, patriotic Australian, and she sees the same in me.” Hanson also used her appearance to highlight the important role WA played in the nation’s economy while also addressing the continued housing crisis in the state.

She said WA was being held back by the federal government. Related Article Political leadership Why these Brisbane Islamic school students spoke to Pauline Hanson “Western Australia helps to pay this nation’s bills. Your royalties fund the services here,” she said. “Your taxes fund them everywhere else. Western Australia is home to roughly one in nine Australians, but generates one in $6 of national economic output. “Western Australia generates almost one in $5 of national business investment. It generates almost half of the country’s exported goods.

Advertisement

“But your businesses are being held back by this government.” Hanson pointed to Perth’s rental vacancy rate of 0.6 per cent – the tightest capital city in the country after Darwin – and blamed the federal government’s migration policy for the increase. “Families with nowhere to live in the richest state in the nation. And let me tell you why there’s nowhere to live, it’s not a mystery,” she said. “Last year, this country’s population grew by more than 400,000 people. In the same year, we finished about 173,000 homes, nearly 2½ people for every new home, and it’s not slowing down. “In just four years, this government will have added almost 1.6 million people through net overseas migration. Nearly one million arrived in 2022 and 2023 alone.

“This government is running the biggest migration program in our history, and building nowhere near the homes to match it. Then it acts surprised when your kids can’t afford a house and services are overwhelmed. “That’s not compassion. That’s negligence.” More to come.

title: Double blow for Rabbitohs after Murray, Mitchell injury prognoses revealed
text:
South Sydney superstar Latrell Mitchell may not play again this year, in a second blow to the Rabbitohs’ finals chances after captain Cameron Murray succumbed to an ankle injury.

Murray suffered the injury in the loss to Canberra on Saturday night and is expected to be sidelined for up to a month. The loss of the NSW forward is set to be compounded by the news that Mitchell is struggling to return to the field due to his own injury issues.

Cameron Murray and Latrell Mitchell, Souths’ highest-paid players, are due for more time on the sidelines.

Mitchell’s scheduled return from a back injury last month was aborted after he tore his calf in training. It was hoped Mitchell could be fit for the round-23 clash against Parramatta on August 8, but there is no guarantee he will play again this year.

The loss of the star duo is a huge blow for the Rabbitohs, who are in seventh position on the ladder and fighting for one of the final spots in the top eight.

text:

Manhattan Gold Corporation has unearthed a swag of high-grade gold results from first-pass sampling at its newly acquired and never-before-drilled Vanquish prospect in Nunavut, Canada, hitting a hefty 20 grams per tonne (g/t) gold in testing. The results from a quickfire channel and grab sampling program at the prospect within the company’s expansive Hook Lake project included impressive channel assays of 18.55g/t gold over 0.3 metres and 15.45g/t gold over 0.4m. Manhattan Gold’s Hook Lake project area in Nunavut, Canada. The new work at Vanquish, which sits 80 kilometres west of Manhattan’s flagship Jaws target, has now identified gold mineralisation over at least 131 metres of strike, significantly expanding a footprint previously defined by historic surface trenching that returned 5.75g/t gold over 1.5m. Manhattan only recently acquired the prospect in April after a review of historic data identified the biggest gold-arsenic till anomaly within the Kaminak greenstone belt, which forms part of the broader Rankin Ennadai greenstone belt.

Advertisement

‘Drilling at Jaws is advancing,samples from the initial holes are being analysed and first assay results are expected shortly.’ Manhattan Gold Corporation tecnical advisor Eric Sondergaard Despite the compelling geochemical smoke, the target has never been kissed by the drill bit. The company says the reconnaissance sampling at Vanquish was completed during the mobilisation of the drill rig and supporting equipment to Jaws. Vanquish is one of 12 prospects within Manhattan’s 665-square-kilometre Hook Lake project, which sits in the underexplored but highly prospective Rankin-Ennadai greenstone belt. The belt is also home to mining giant Agnico Eagle’s 6.7-million-ounce Meliadine operation, 225 kilometres to the northeast and one of Canada’s largest gold mines. The company says this underlines the world-class gold endowment of the region and suggests it has the potential for further significant deposits. Manhattan Gold Corporation technical advisor Eric Sondergaard said: “The early results from Vanquish are particularly exciting. This is a newly acquired, never- drilled prospect where a limited first-pass program has already delivered grades of up to 20g/t gold and confirmed mineralisation across 131 metres of strike.”

Advertisement

Manhattan’s current work is focused on a maiden reverse circulation program at its flagship Jaws target, where drilling in the 1980’s outlined a historical foreign resource estimate of 3.4 million tonnes grading 2.38g/t gold for a contained 285,000 ounces. The company is quick to say that the estimate is not compliant with the modern JORC code and it is now eagerly awaiting the first batch of assays from its Jaws drilling program to firm up the numbers for conversion into a JORC-compliant inventory. First results are expected in the coming weeks. Beyond Jaws and Vanquish, the Hook Lake project hosts a grab-bag of other compelling targets. These include the never-drilled Quantum and Lotus prospects, which have returned surface gold samples of 16.75g/t and 8.01g/t respectively. Manhattan’s other key target is Spectre, a high-grade polymetallic Volcanogenic Massive Sulphide (VMS) system where drilling in the 1970’s hit a mouthwatering 10.51m at 2.91 per cent copper, 6.7 per cent zinc and 95.67g/t silver. The company plans to integrate the new high-grade gold results with recently acquired magnetic and LiDAR data to refine the structural model and prioritise drill targets at Vanquish.

Advertisement

With the drill rods turning at its flagship Jaws prospect and a brand new, high-grade discovery at Vanquish screaming to be tested, Manhattan appears to be systematically building a serious inventory of gold targets. Punters will likely be watching to see if the drill bit at Jaws can deliver what the surface rocks at Vanquish are already suggesting. Is your ASX-listed company doing something interesting? title: Shallow gold hits put Pacgold on fast track to SA mine restart
text:
Advertisement

Sponsored BusinessCompaniesBulls N' Bears Shallow gold hits put Pacgold on fast track to SA mine restart Brought to you by BULLS N’ BEARS Penny Taylor July 21, 2026 — 12:31pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Pacgold has unearthed significant shallow oxide gold on the edge of the historical Vertigo open pit at its wholly owned White Dam project in South Australia, potentially putting the mine’s restart on the fast track. The recently completed reverse circulation drilling began with an initial 178-hole campaign before a successful first phase prompted a further 102-hole infill and extension program. The latest round was designed to upgrade much of Vertigo’s inferred resource into the higher-confidence indicated category, strengthening pit design, reserve estimation and mine planning. Aerial view showing the Vertigo deposit (bottom left), the Hannaford pit and existing heap-leach pad (upper centre), and the operating plant and SART facility (upper right) at Pacgold’s White Dam gold project in South Australia. The program delivered some impressive shallow hits, with highlights including 4 metres grading 3.1 grams per tonne (g/t) gold from surface, including a 1m slice grading 7.2g/t gold. Other notable results delivered 6m grading 2.2g/t gold from surface, 11m grading 1.2g/t gold also from surface, 17m grading 0.9g/t gold from 6m and 13m grading 1.3g/t gold from a depth of 14m.

Advertisement

‘The development from ore discovered north and east of the pit will drastically reduce pre-strip costs and bring forward gold production.’ Pacgold managing director Matthew Boyes Beyond the headline intersections, the final assays also confirmed shallow northern and western extensions to the Vertigo deposit outside the existing resource model, pointing to further resource growth. The results will now be incorporated into an updated resource estimate for Vertigo, currently being prepared by external consultants and slated for delivery early in the September quarter. Prior to the update, the White Dam project hosted a global resource of 4.6 million tonnes grading 0.7g/t gold for 102,000 ounces across the Vertigo, Hannaford and White Dam North deposits. Vertigo is the first of White Dam’s four deposits targeted for restart, putting it at the front of the queue to bring White Dam back into production. Management believes the shallow oxide mineralisation will improve both the economics and timing of bringing Vertigo back into production.

Advertisement

Pacgold managing director Matthew Boyes said: “These excellent shallow oxide intervals represent a very material win for Pacgold in respect to fast-tracking the restart of the Vertigo mine and getting shallow low-cost oxide dirt onto the old pad while extending the ore leaching capacity with the new pad expansion.” The company is already pushing forward with a heap-leach pad expansion, with engineering nearing completion ahead of construction tenders. The bigger plant will allow for additional oxide ore to be processed as operations ramp up. Since the mineralisation is shallow, oxidised and beside an existing pit, Pacgold believes it’s ideally suited for low-cost heap-leach processing, where a cyanide solution recovers gold without the expense of conventional milling. That winning combination could slash pre-strip waste removal, reduce working capital and bring forward the first new ounces of gold. Management says the focus will shortly shift to pit optimisation, mine design and cashflow modelling once the new resource model is complete. The rig has now moved on from Vertigo, with drilling already underway at White Dam North ahead of a spell at the nearby Rolling and Hannaford deposits. All three are expected to provide additional resource growth and future ore sources for the planned restart. The company is also preparing regional exploration drilling at the Wadnaminga, Wilkins, Green and Gold and Mary Mine prospects later this quarter as it hunts for additional ore sources within easy trucking distance of White Dam.

Advertisement

For a brownfield restart project such as White Dam, the gold’s proximity to the plant matters far more than the grades returned. Between 2010 and 2018, White Dam produced 180,000 ounces. It already has established open pits, a heap-leach facility, a processing plant and all the supporting infrastructure a new miner could want. Much of the operation was paid for by previous owners, allowing Pacgold to bring the project back into production far faster and cheaper than building a mine from scratch. The company’s efforts to restart mining in South Australia seem well-timed. While gold prices may have pulled back to US$4000 (A$5700) per ounce from their historic highs of more than US$5500 (A$7800) per ounce earlier in the year, major institutions remain bullish. J.P. Morgan has forecast a rebound to an average of A$6,000 per ounce in the final quarter of this year. Finding a source of low-cost, quick-to-mine ore could leave White Dam well placed if bullion regains momentum later this year. Against that backdrop, the White Dam story isn’t about chasing the next big discovery. It’s about systematically unlocking more ounces from an established mining operation and turning yesterday’s infrastructure into tomorrow’s cash flow. The latest drilling has strengthened the investment case for Vertigo to become the first White Dam deposit back in production, while longer term, Pacgold sees White Dam evolving into a regional processing hub. The next catalyst will be the updated resource model and mine plan, which could provide the blueprint for breathing new life into one of South Australia’s proven gold-producing operations. Is your ASX-listed company doing something interesting? Contact: mattbirney@bullsnbears.com.au
