title: Jobs to go at Nine’s metro mastheads in restructure to drive digital push
text:
Advertisement

Breaking BusinessCompaniesMedia & marketing Jobs to go at Nine’s metro mastheads in restructure to drive digital push Calum Jaspan July 21, 2026 — 12:24pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Nine’s publishing division, which includes this masthead, will make dozens of roles redundant as part of a restructure to sharpen its focus on digital news amid a tough advertising market and rapid technological disruption in the media industry. About 30 voluntary redundancies will be initially offered to staff at The Sydney Morning Herald, The Age, WAToday and Brisbane Times as the division pushes for more digital subscribers, with staff in the division’s print operations team and free news site nine.com.au also facing cuts. Nine is announcing job cuts at The Sydney Morning Herald and The Age. Paul Rovere Managing director of the publishing division Tory Maguire informed staff of the upcoming changes in an email and town hall meeting on Tuesday morning, saying that the business needed to adapt to a changing media landscape to protect its journalism. This includes investing in new areas to connect with new audiences, she said. “In particular we need to keep shifting towards the digital-first future,” Maguire said, though she added that print revenue has held up better than previously expected.

Advertisement

Maguire said the company would engage with the Media, Entertainment and Arts Alliance (MEAA) union that represents journalists. Related Article Exclusive

Media & marketing Up to 200 staff to go as Seven’s owner swings the axe Nine signed a landmark deal with Microsoft earlier this month, meaning the tech giant’s AI chatbot Copilot will reference content from the company’s mastheads in its search results, with the media company compensated in return. A briefing from investment bank UBS this month said revenue impact from the Microsoft deal would not have a major impact on the company’s bottom line, suggesting it is worth less than $25 million, but said it was “positive for future potential deals” ahead of expected legislation to bring tech giants to the bargaining table. News organisations around the world are adapting to the rapid uptake of AI chatbots as a tool for consuming information. Google, for example, has rolled out its AI Overview function over the past two years at the top of its search results, leading to a decline in click-through traffic both to news websites and all other web domains. Media union representative Cassie Derrick said the cuts were devastating and called on Nine to give more information about the cuts to the MEAA.

Advertisement

“Nine must engage in genuine consultation with staff through their union, and do everything possible to avoid forced redundancies, including redeployment and voluntary options,” Derrick said. “Newsrooms cannot keep absorbing job cuts without consequences for workloads, diversity, quality journalism and the ability of journalists to hold power to account.” The Australian Financial Review will not be affected by the cuts. Publishing was Nine’s second best-performing division in its 2025 full-year results, with revenue of $526 million and earnings, before a common range of deductions, of $153 million. Declines to print sales were offset by subscriber growth, the company said in its annual report last year. Projections show the division’s earnings are expected to be flat in the upcoming 2026 results, delivered in August. The company took $33 million in costs out of the division in the 2025 financial year. Nine has since sold its share of digital real estate business Domain for $1.4 billion, as well as its talkback radio division. It also purchased outdoor media company QMS for $850 million. title: Trump wants to make his trade wall bigger and better than ever before
text:
Advertisement

BusinessMarketsTrade wars Opinion Trump wants to make his trade wall bigger and better than ever before Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist July 21, 2026 — 12:04pm

July 21, 2026 — 12:04pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

As Donald Trump’s latest attempt to rebuild the tariff wall demolished by the US Supreme Court earlier this year goes live, he’s already planning to make it higher. This was always going to be a significant week for global trade, given that Trump’s temporary replacements for the “Liberation Day” tariffs – the 10 per cent baseline tariffs on almost all imports to the US, which the Supreme Court had ruled illegal in February – are scheduled to expire on Friday. Those replacement tariffs, using Section 122 of the Trade Act, have themselves been ruled illegal by US courts and the revenue raised may have to be reimbursed. In any event, they could only be imposed for a maximum of 150 days. US President Donald Trump is working to build a tariff wall the likes of which the world has never seen before. Bloomberg That period ends on Friday, and the Trump administration has been racing to get a more permanent replacement in place.

Advertisement

Last month, using a more robust section of the Trade Act, Section 301 (although it, too, might be challenged in the courts) the administration announced it would impose new tariffs of either 10 per cent or 12.5 per cent on 99.4 per cent of all imports to the US, using the pretext that it was a response to inadequate efforts by its trading partners, including Australia, to prevent imports made with forced labour. Separately, it identified 16 of America’s biggest trading partners – including China, Japan, the European Union, India and South Korea – as potential targets for even higher tariffs, claiming they had excess manufacturing capacity and production that burdened or restricted US commerce. Trump’s flurry of trade-related actions over the past week shows how committed he is to his misconceived understanding of trade. Last Friday, Trump announced even more tariffs, this time on Brazil, as retaliation for “unfair” trade practices, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio saying Brazil hadn’t negotiated in good faith and that President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva was “putting his own ego ahead of making a deal”, which is a bit rich coming from the Trump administration. Trump originally hit Brazil with 50 per cent tariffs as retaliation for the prosecution of former Brazilian president Jair Bolsonaro, a Trump friend and ally. Those were knocked out by the Supreme Court.

Advertisement

It is revealing – and shows the limitations of Trump’s trade wars – that the latest tariffs will only cover about a third of Brazil’s exports to the US, exempting coffee, orange juice, beef, nuts, honey, tropical fruit, iron and manganese ore, wood pulp, oil and oil products and civil aircraft, among a long list. Related Article Pollution Trump blames Canada for wildfire smoke as US forests also burn The initial tariffs, while they were in place, sent the price of coffee and other groceries soaring in the US, causing a consumer backlash. The administration is clearly trying to avoid a repeat of that experience. There are some things that the US doesn’t or can’t produce itself, or where it can’t produce enough to meet its domestic demand, or where other countries can produce it more cheaply – something Trump has consistently failed to recognise in his trade wars with the rest of the world. As if the imminent introduction of the Section 301 tariffs, and the renewed but relatively limited attack on Brazil’s exports weren’t sufficient, on Monday Trump announced new 50 per cent tariffs on Canada, to take effect in 30 days.

Advertisement

Those tariffs are supposedly in response to the actions of Canadian provinces in halting purchases of US alcohol, putting tariffs on US cars and discriminating against US dairy products. Trump is, however, still seething over the fact that Canada was one of only two countries – China was the other – that retaliated when he imposed his Liberation Day tariffs. Last week, he even threatened another tariff because wildfires in Canada had resulted in the US being “invaded” by dirty air and pollution, saying the cost of the “pollution” should be added to the existing tariffs. The fact that Canada issued air quality warnings of its own, because of the smoke from US wildfires, seems to have escaped him. Donald Trump’s administration said Canada was one of only a few countries that had retaliated against US tariffs. AP Trump’s latest tariffs will apply to a wide range of Canadian exports, including those covered by the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA). In previous rounds of tariffs, goods complying with the free trade agreement were exempted.

Advertisement

Trump has, of course, given notice that the US will exit the USMCA (which he negotiated in 2018) when it expires in a decade, unless Mexico and Canada negotiate a new deal more favourable to the US. A third round of negotiations with Mexico is scheduled this week. So far, Canada has had no serious engagement with the US. Related Article Opinion

Trump's White House Trump’s three-pronged threat to American democracy Peter Hartcher Political and international editor The new tariffs could be a crude attempt to gain some leverage over Canada to force it to the table, although its Prime Minister Mark Carney has shown little interest in making concessions to the US. That the tariffs conflict with the USMCA could easily lead to a deeper trade conflict between the countries, even as Trump weighs up the prospect of reopening hostilities with the European Union.

Advertisement

Officials from his administration are putting together a list of Spanish goods the US could embargo in retaliation for Spain’s refusal to agree to spend 5 per cent of its GDP on defence, which Trump has insisted on. Spain’s government also stopped the US from using military bases within Spain to launch attacks on Iran. Earlier this month, at the NATO summit, Trump said he hadn’t spoken to Spain. “Spain is a wasted cause,” he said. “We don’t want to any trade business with Spain any more.” He also instructed US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to cut off trade with Spain. “Take care of ⁠it immediately. Don’t even talk to them. They’re hopeless. They’re bad people,” Trump said. “They make so much money with us, and we’re going to see that they make a lot less.” Trump tried to bask in the reflected glory of Spain’s World Cup success, but otherwise he’s not a fan of the country. Getty Images

Advertisement

The two countries actually don’t trade much with each other – the US imported about $US21 billion of Spanish goods last year and exported about $US26 billion to Spain, so it has a trade surplus with the country – but targeting Spain could blow up the deal the US finally signed with the EU late last month. The EU takes the view that a threat to one of its 27 members is a threat to all – so, if Trump’s threats against Spain are implemented, the wider trade deal could be jeopardised. The new tariffs, which may or may not survive the inevitable legal challenges from US importers, are Trump’s final attempt to rebuild his tariff wall through a patchwork of legislative headings that have never previously been used in the way that he proposes. Related Article Opinion

Trade wars Trump blows up the ‘best and most important deal ever made’ Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist The statutory authority for the new Canadian tariffs, for instance, is Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930, which essentially boils down to the preferential treatment Canada supposedly gives to third countries relative to its treatment of the US. It has never been used before.

Advertisement

It is conceivable that the Section 301 tariffs – which were intended, it seems, for use in bilateral disputes rather than to provide cover for a global tariff regime – could be demolished by the courts. It’s also possible that the Democrats gain control of Congress at the midterm elections and try to rein in Trump’s trade aggression. Trump’s flurry of trade-related actions over the past week, however, shows how committed he is to his misconceived understanding of trade. They also highlight his wilful ignorance of who eventually pays his tariffs and the reality that – despite his trade deals and promises that they would lead to massive investment in US manufacturing and a surge in well-paid manufacturing jobs – the manufacturing sector in the US continues to shrink, along with its jobs and real wages. title: ‘It’s people’s jobs’: Dragons take aim at costly refereeing calls
text:
Advertisement

SportNRLNRL 2026 ‘It’s people’s jobs’: Dragons take aim at costly refereeing calls Chris Barrett July 21, 2026 — 11:59am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

St George Illawarra have raised concerns with the NRL about a series of refereeing calls that have gone against them over the past month, with club chief Tim Watsford saying they had potential consequences “beyond the football field”. The last-placed Dragons have been far more competitive under Dean Young, who was on Monday handed the full-time head coaching job and a two-year contract. Dean Young was chosen ahead of 2010 Dragons premiership teammate Ben Hornby. Getty Images He was appointed despite the team being victorious in only two of 10 games under his tenure, a nod to the marked improvement they have made since he took over from Shane Flanagan in April following a winless start to the season. It was an acknowledgement from Dragons officials of Young’s “all-in” vision for the club and the confidence he had instilled in the line-up, but also a string of results impacted by refereeing.

Advertisement

Captain Damian Cook unloaded on the officiating after a 20-12 defeat to the Warriors on Saturday in which they were on the wrong side of three key decisions, querying whether their lowly position on the ladder worked against them. The Dragons also felt aggrieved at their treatment in close losses to Canberra and Newcastle, as well as the contentious denial of a solo try to Cook against Penrith at Magic Round. Loading “As a club, you’re kind of looked at by wins or losses; I’m not shying away from that, that’s the game we live in. But you could argue there’s three, possibly four games in Dean’s tenure that very much could have been a W next to it versus the L,” Watsford said. “That’s where pressure comes. This is beyond the football field a little bit; it’s got people’s jobs [at stake]. Dean’s been auditioning with some pretty ordinary calls. I called out a couple in Canberra and obviously on the weekend we saw a few, and there was a couple in Newcastle.”

Advertisement

Young beat long-time friend and 2010 premiership teammate Ben Hornby to the post in what Watsford indicated was a heavily subscribed application process. The Dragons CEO said Young was not chosen on the basis of wins and losses during the period in which he had been interim coach, but the club had written to the NRL repeatedly “to ensure that we’ve got our foot at the table”. The Dragons and their fans will be hoping for more success in 2027. Getty Images Young himself said he was more focused on Sunday’s match against 16th-placed Gold Coast Titans, which the Dragons would need to come out on top of to have any chance of avoiding the wooden spoon. As for next year, the club will welcome Keaon Koloamatangi, Scott Drinkwater, Connor Watson and Luke Metcalf, and Watsford made it clear that qualifying for the finals was a reasonable expectation.

Advertisement

Asked about making the top eight in 2027, Young said: “Look, with finals, [only] eight teams can make it ... so you’ve got to do a lot of things right. But if I don’t believe that we can make the finals next year, how are my players going to believe that? Related Article NRL 2026 Officials got three of seven calls wrong at the weekend. Apart from that, the NRL was happy “There’s a lot of work to be done, there’s a pre-season to be planned, but at the moment it’s this week against the Titans to make sure that we continue the consistency that we’ve shown in the last five or six weeks.” Young and father Craig have become one of a select few father-and-son combinations to be first-grade coaches of a club. Others include Don and David Furner with the Raiders, John and Stuart Raper with Cronulla, and Allan and Craig Fitzgibbon, also at the Sharks.

Advertisement

“It’s a very proud moment for myself, but also I understand the responsibility and the pressure that comes with this role,” he said. “I wanted to be a first-grade coach ever since I started back in 2013, and I’m thankful to the club to show the support and give me the opportunity. I’ve got two years to make the most of it, and that’s what I plan on doing.”

title: ‘Evasive’ postie who drove to KFC on lunch break loses job
text:
Advertisement

BusinessWorkplaceUnfair dismissal ‘Evasive’ postie who drove to KFC on lunch break loses job Millie Muroi July 21, 2026 — 11:47am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

A postie who drove his Australia Post truck to KFC on his lunch break without permission and admitted to being “evasive” about his whereabouts during work hours, has failed to win back his job after being sacked. The Newcastle man, who was employed by Australia Post for more than three decades before losing his job last year, said his dismissal was harsh, unjust and unreasonable, but was ultimately found to have engaged in serious and wilful misconduct by the country’s fair work umpire. A postal driver in NSW was dismissed after taking an Australia Post van to KFC on his lunch break without permission. AP That included being absent from duty for more than an hour on May 7 last year, and during that time, using an Australia Post vehicle for a non-work purpose – by his account, driving to KFC – without seeking permission and failing to tell his supervisor where he was or what he was doing. While he was entitled to a 30-minute unpaid lunch break, the driver did not take that break at the specified time of 10.40am. “[He] was required to let his supervisor know if he was taking a lunch break at a different time or location,” Fair Work Commission deputy president Tony Saunders said.

Advertisement

Related Article Unfair dismissal Chauffeur who agreed to retire at 65 to be paid $40,000 after ageist dismissal On the same day, the driver also left work 30 minutes early without seeking permission. Australia Post alleged the man was unreasonable, disrespectful, and acted inappropriately when asked by his supervisor to account for where he had been: first, refusing to speak, then telling his supervisor to “make an appointment” if speaking to him during work hours – a requirement Saunders said was unreasonable. When the driver refused to answer the question about his whereabouts, his supervisor asked: “why are you trying to be ...” While the supervisor had paused and was thinking about how to end the question, the driver offered, “evasive?” to which the supervisor responded: “yeah. That’s the perfect word.” Australia Post also alleged the driver had told his supervisor that he was “going to have some fun for the next two years.” When asked what he meant, the driver allegedly said: “I probably have another two years left before I retire.”

Advertisement

While the postie denied this exchange had occurred, the commission preferred Australia Post’s evidence on the alleged discussion, with Saunders saying the driver did “have some fun” by “playing games” with the company. Those games included replying to the company’s queries after hours when he knew the work phone was not being manned, and blocking text messages about his non-attendance. Australia Post also claimed the driver failed to attend work or to contact them regarding absences from work on at least 19 occasions over the course of a month, constituting repeated failures to follow reasonable directions. The Newcastle man was employed by Australia Post for more than three decades before losing his job last year. Jason South During that month, the man’s supervisor and several other managers sent about 17 text messages to his phone, on an almost daily basis because he had not attended work or said he would be absent, requesting him to call work to discuss the situation. “[The man’s] evidence in relation to these text messages was not credible,” Saunders said. “He said he thought the text messages might have been a scam, but ...[he] could not give a satisfactory explanation as to why he did not simply call his workplace to check whether the text messages were genuine.”

Advertisement

Related Article Exclusive

Workplace No hiring if an AI can do the job, staff at mining giant told The driver had been diagnosed as having “acute stress reaction” and had therefore been rostered on to work with reduced time pressure and lower volume. Medical certificates presented to the commission also showed he was suffering from mental health difficulties, which Saunders acknowledged had impacted the way in which the driver responded to the directions and requests from his employer. But Saunders said while the driver may have trouble finding an alternate job given his age and long employment history in one job, and would possibly face a difficult time from a financial perspective, his conduct was of “such a grave nature” as to be “repugnant” to the employment relationship. “A meaningful employment relationship cannot exist when an employee demonstrates an ongoing failure and refusal to co-operate with their employer,” he said. A spokesperson for Australia Post said the company welcomed the Fair Work Commission’s finding.



“We play an important role in delivering for the community and have high standards of conduct for all team members,” they said. The Business Briefing newsletter delivers major stories, exclusive coverage and expert opinion. Sign up to get it every weekday morning.

title: 'Kings of the World': Spain's World Cup stars get a hero's welcome from fans and royals
text:
In Brief Spain's players presented the World Cup winner's trophy to the Spanish Royal Family.

Some players, like forward Borja Iglesias, played music on a DJ set on top of the parade bus.

New World Cup champions Spain enjoyed a vibrant celebration parade on an open-top bus through the streets of Madrid on Monday evening, with a jubilant crowd of an estimated two million people out to celebrate, according to local authorities.

The players, some shirtless, others holding drinks, set off on their procession under a blazing sun, along the main arteries in the city centre where a sea of fans in red and yellow were waiting, before arriving at Cibeles square where they took to the stage and partied into the night.

The squad and coach Luis de la Fuente were introduced one by one to the fans, each with their own choice of music that they sung along to, wearing "We are champions" shirts.

"(Spain) showed the world what it is to work together, with one mind and one heart," said pop superstar Shakira in a video message played to the team and crowd.

De la Fuente offered a similar message, hailing his players as the "best in the world" and telling fans not to forget that "together we are stronger".

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

After flying back from the United States to Madrid, the players were received by King Felipe VI and the royal family earlier Monday, and then Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez Pérez-Castejón, before kicking off the parade.

Spain's players paraded onboard an open-top bus through the streets of Madrid. Source: AFP / Cesar Manso

Players passed around the gleaming World Cup trophy and waved to their adoring supporters, some lining balconies, others on top of bus stops, the day after defeating Argentina 1-0 in extra-time in New Jersey.

Ferran Torres's strike in extra-time was enough to bring Spain their second World Cup, following a first victory in 2010.

Authorities estimated that around 120,000 fans were gathered at Cibeles square alone, where the celebrations reached their climax. Source: AFP / Pierre-Philippe Marcou

"You have shown your grit, your resilience and your courage ... combining head and heart," the king told La Roja's stars, who were dressed in suits but changed to more casual clothing for their parade on a hot summer's day.

"Kings of the world" ran the words on the front of their vehicle, with fans chanting "champions" to their heroes.

Spain captain Rodri held the World Cup trophy aloft at the top of the stairs as he descended from the Iberia plane that flew the team home after clinching their second star.

Later at Moncloa, Manchester City midfielder Rodri said this was the crowning achievement of his career.

"I've always said it, winning with your country is the most beautiful thing there is, above all a World Cup," said Rodri.

"This generation grew up watching (Iker) Casillas and (Andres) Iniesta lift the cup and that we can do it today is the biggest thing you can do in football."

Many younger supporters were not yet born when Iniesta's extra-time goal against the Netherlands secured Spain's first World Cup in South Africa 16 years ago.

"I'm really happy about the second star" Spain has earned, "very joyful," said Hector Mollar, a 14-year-old student, who said he was able to see Torres, Lamine Yamal and Mikel Merino as the bus parade passed.

It is "very different" to live a day like today than to have someone tell you about it, Hector added.

Match-winner Torres joked he was "flying, literally" after the team returned from the United States after beating Argentina in New Jersey.

title: Legendary media figure Derryn Hinch makes his final headlines with state funeral
text:
in brief Derryn Hinch was honoured with a state funeral in Melbourne on Monday.

Mourners gathered to express their condolences and share stories about the bombastic figure.

Derryn Hinch has been remembered for making waves as one of Australia's most outspoken and influential voices.

Longtime friend and colleague Darren James led mourners through a state funeral for the former broadcaster and senator on Monday.

"He was a journalist, a newspaper reporter, a TV presenter, a godfather, an author, a senator and, a prisoner ... he did it all," James told the service.

"Some people pass through life without leaving a ripple, he created tsunamis."

Hinch died on 10 July at the age of 82 after a lengthy health struggle, much of which he documented for followers on social media.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

The service for the lifelong atheist, who went to jail for identifying a paedophile Catholic priest before his trial, was held at the Timber Yard events space in Port Melbourne, near factories and apartments devoid of any religious leaning.

It attracted notable figures, including news presenters Ray Martin and Denis Walter, entertainers Rhonda Burchmore and Greg Evans, and former Crown executive Ann Peacock.

Other mourners, including Labor MP Danny Pearson, who attended on behalf of Premier Jacinta Allan, streamed in to pay their respects.

"Not all of us knew him personally, but for many it felt like we did," Pearson said.

An Australian flag was draped over his casket, surrounded by vases of red roses symbolising deep love, respect, courage and enduring affection.

Victorian MP Danny Pearson spoke at Derryn Hinch's state funeral, appearing on behalf of Premier Jacinta Allan. Source: AAP / Eddie Jim

Hinch initially moved from New Zealand to Australia to pursue a career in journalism, climbing the ranks to become editor of The Sun before turning to a career in radio.

He became known as the "human headline" — a moniker he enthusiastically adopted — for his outspoken and often controversial opinions.

Former 3AW colleagues Denis O'Kane and Tony Tardio spoke of their love for Hinch, who was well known for his hatred of child sex offenders and those who shielded them.

"There will never be another like him, even his name was unique," Tardio said.

"He was a journalistic James Bond, drama followed him everywhere — even to his first wedding. And then he went on to marry three other times."

His ex-wife and friend, actor Jacki Weaver, sent a video tribute from America describing Hinch as a wonderful stepfather and companion.

She also referenced Hinch's reputation as a notorious ladies' man.

Derryn Hinch worked across the Australian media landscape, and many of his former colleagues' spoke of their love for the broadcaster during his service. Source: AAP / Christopher Hopkins

"I'd like to pay my respects to all the women who loved Derryn, and who loved him back," she said.

"I won't name them all ... we don't have time."

While commanding the airwaves for decades as a talkback radio host on Melbourne radio station 3AW, Hinch became known for his uninhibited takes and a tendency to court controversy.

Later, Hinch turned to the political arena, forming Derryn Hinch's Justice Party and serving as a senator for Victoria in the federal parliament from 2016 to 2019.

His party focused on reforms to the justice system, including better protections for children, tightening of bail and parole laws, and the establishment of a sex offenders' register.

Hinch's final farewell ended with a eulogy by fellow media stalwart Martin, who said his mate would have loved the idea of having a state funeral and a standing ovation.

The service was brought to a close with the music of That's Life by Frank Sinatra, often used as the sign-off from his daily radio show.

title: Changing of the guard: The young stars taking over from Messi, Ronaldo, and Neymar
text:
Key Points Spain's 2026 World Cup win closed the book on football's golden generation, with Messi, Ronaldo and Neymar, all bowing out empty-handed.

A new wave of young stars is now poised to inherit the game.

When Spanish winger Ferran Torres swept the ball past Argentine goalkeeper Emiliano Martínez in the 106th minute at New York New Jersey Stadium on Monday morning, he did more than win Spain a second World Cup title. He drew the curtain on an era.

Lionel Messi, 39, wept after the match; beaten in a final for the first time and denied the chance to claim the tournament's Golden Boot for top goal-scorer from France's Kylian Mbappé.

Cristiano Ronaldo's Portugal had long since gone home, the 41-year-old's World Cup days now behind him. Neymar, 34, patched together for one last Brazilian campaign, could not drag his side past the Round of 16.

Croatia's Luka Modrić, 40, and veteran goalkeepers Manuel Neuer, 40, (Germany) and Guillermo Ochoa, 41 (Mexico) were among other members of a 'golden generation' that took its collective bow in North America, and almost every one of them left empty-handed.

For all its nostalgia, the 2026 tournament was equally an audition. So, who inherits the game now that these legends have moved on?

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Here are the names to build the next decade around.

Lamine Yamal (Spain)

He came within a fingertip of settling the final, a curling stoppage-time free-kick drawing the save of the tournament from Martínez.

Lamine Yamal, 19, was the creative heartbeat of Spain's 2026 World Cup triumph. Source: AAP / Alexandre Martins/DPPI/IPA Sport 2/ipa-agency.net//Sipa USA

Across all seven games he was the creative heartbeat of the world champions, drifting in from the right flank to pull defences apart and set the tempo of Spain's attack.

Barcelona's number 10 already had a domestic treble and a European Championship to his name. Now, at just 19, he adds a world title — a haul most players never assemble across an entire career, let alone before turning 20.

Pau Cubarsí (Spain)

Arguably Spain's most important player, the 19-year-old centre-back anchored a defence that conceded just once all tournament. His six clean sheets in seven matches made him the fastest to that mark in World Cup history, past a record Italy's Paolo Maldini set in 1990.

Pau Cubarsí, 19, anchored a Spain defence that conceded just once en route to the 2026 world title. Source: AAP / Cody Grubbs/Sipa USA

He shackled Mbappé for 90 minutes in the semi-final, yet it's his passing that sets him apart: no player sent more balls into the opposition half.

Cubarsí's performances earned him the FIFA Young Player Award at the tournament.

A LaLiga regular for Barcelona since before he turned 17, he reads danger a decade ahead of his years. Sky Sports UK pundits are already tipping him to become a future top-five centre-back of all time.

Gilberto Mora (Mexico)

The youngest player at the entire tournament at 17, he grew into it superbly, running the midfield against Ecuador in the Round of 32 as the youngest man to start a knockout tie since Pelé — orchestrating a composed 2-0 win.

Gilberto Mora, 17, was the youngest player at the 2026 World Cup and starred in Mexico's midfield. Source: AAP / Ismael Rosas/ Eyepix Group/Sipa USA

The Club Tijuana playmaker, who operates as a left-footed attacking midfielder, is already the youngest goalscorer in Liga MX history and arrived having become the youngest player ever to win a senior international trophy — the 2025 Gold Cup, at 16.

Real Madrid, PSG, Chelsea, and Barcelona have all been linked; with a release clause reported around €20m ($32m), a move to Europe feels a matter of when, not if.

Yan Diomande (Côte d'Ivoire)

The breakout player of the group stage, he set up Nicolas Pépé's opener against Curaçao to send Côte d'Ivoire into the knockouts for the first time ever, and became the only player this century to complete 10 dribbles and create 10 chances across his first three World Cup games.

Yan Diomande, 19, was one of the breakout stars of the 2026 World Cup, helping Côte d'Ivoire reach the knockouts for the first time. Source: AAP / Baptiste Fernandez/Icon Sport/Sipa USA

All raw pace and fearless dribbling, the 19-year-old winger plays in Germany for RB Leipzig, where he was named Bundesliga Rookie of the Season after 12 goals and eight assists — form he carried in from a standout run at the Africa Cup of Nations earlier this year.

Liverpool and Barcelona are circling a player now valued past €100 million ($163 million).

Lucas Herrington (Australia)

The tournament's most unlikely riser. The 18-year-old centre-back made his World Cup debut in the final group game against Paraguay and put in a composed 90-minute shift that helped Australia to a clean sheet and into the Round of 32. In doing so, he became the youngest Socceroo ever to start a World Cup match and the youngest player in the US domestic league, the MLS, to feature at a World Cup.

Lucas Herrington, 18, became the youngest Socceroo to play at a World Cup. Source: AAP / LVDIDI/SPP/Sipa USA

Barely known outside the A-League a year ago, a January move to the Colorado Rapids changed everything: he walked straight into the starting eleven and has played every minute since.

At 6'4" and unflappable, he's already had a bid by Barcelona rejected by his club, and Liverpool are reportedly scouting him closely. A defensive cornerstone in the making.

Patrick Beach (Australia)

Herrington's compatriot and the goalkeeping sensation few saw coming.

Patrick Beach, 22, kept a clean sheet on his World Cup debut as Australia beat Türkiye 2-0. Source: AAP / Bob Frid/EPA

On his World Cup debut — controversially picked ahead of veteran captain Mat Ryan — the 22-year-old kept a clean sheet and made eight saves, including a full-stretch tip onto the post, in a stunning 2-0 win over Türkiye.

Beach became a household name overnight, and has already parlayed it into a five-year move from Melbourne City to newly promoted French first division side Troyes.

Warren Zaïre-Emery (France)

The 20-year-old PSG midfielder marshalled France's engine room while on the pitch, combining tireless pressing with a composure that belies his age.

Warren Zaïre-Emery, 20, featured for France on their run to the 2026 World Cup bronze-medal match. Source: AAP / Tom Weller/dpa/picture-alliance/Sipa USA

Already a two-time Champions League winner and serial Ligue 1 champion in Paris, he is touted as a future France captain — the natural successor to a storied generation of French midfielders.

Désiré Doué (France)

He made his mark whether starting or off the bench: a headed goal against Norway from a Bradley Barcola cross, and an assist for Mbappé against Morocco, across six lively appearances.

Désiré Doué, 21, was among France's brightest young talents at the 2026 World Cup. Source: AAP / Tom Weller/dpa/picture-alliance/Sipa USA

The 21-year-old PSG forward starred in the 2025 Champions League final, scoring twice in the win over Inter Milan, and arrived in North America off a 13-goal, 11-assist club season.

Two-footed, press-resistant and fearless, he is fast becoming undroppable for club and country.

Ayyoub Bouaddi (Morocco)

He anchored one of the tournament's great runs, starting all but one match as the Atlas Lions reached the quarter-finals for a second successive World Cup.

Ayyoub Bouaddi, 18, anchored Morocco's midfield as the Atlas Lions reached the 2026 World Cup quarter-finals. Source: AAP / Baptiste Fernandez/Icon Sport/Sipa USA

The opener against Brazil was his statement game, dictating play against Casemiro, Bruno Guimarães and Lucas Paquetá with 91 percent passing accuracy at just 18.

The Lille midfielder committed to Morocco over France only this year. Manchester City are now pushing hardest to sign him, ahead of Arsenal and Manchester United, with Lille holding out for around €100 million ($163 million). New City boss Enzo Maresca is reportedly keen to bring him straight into the Etihad squad rather than send him back on loan.

title: The World Cup is over. Here's where your football fix comes from next
text:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup might be over, but the beautiful game lives on, and there's plenty more football to sink your teeth into.

A cumulative 19 million Australians have tuned in to watch the World Cup over the course of the tournament, among them new fans who might be searching for a new team or a new hero player to follow.

Australia's top-flight A League men's competition will resume on 16 October.

Here's what's coming up before then.

EU and US comps underway and to come

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

As the northern hemisphere's summer draws to an end, players return to the fields and punters fill the stands in national competitions.

In Europe, Norway's Eliteserien is already in its sixth round and will run until December, with players such as Patrick Berg having almost no time to recover after making the World Cup quarter-finals in a historic run.

Fans hoping to get more of the charismatic Erling Haaland will have to wait until the English Premier League (EPL) kicks off on 21 August.

Haaland plays for Manchester City, along with the World Cup-winning Spanish captain Rodri, who was also awarded the tournament's best player.

Spain's La Liga starts on 15 August.

This star-studded comp is where you can watch 19-year-old Lamine Yamal, crucial goalscorer Ferran Torres, and Pedri take the field for Barcelona off the back of their iconic win against Argentina.

The Socceroos' Mat Ryan is expected to take the pitch with Levante while France's two-time World Cup Golden Boot winner, Kylian Mbappé, plays for Real Madrid.

The Italian Serie A and the French Ligue 1 will both start on 23 August.

In Italy we can expect to see Socceroos Alessandro Circati and Christian Volpato play for Parma and Sassuolo respectively.

The United States' Major League Soccer tournament has already started — tune in there to watch living legend Lionel Messi and fellow Argentine Rodrigo De Paul play for Inter Miami and Australia's Lucas Herrington make an impact with the Colorado Rapids.

Champions League

The European UEFA Champions League is considered the most prestigious annual tournament in international football.

The 36-team league is made up of the best clubs on the continent, and it's one way you can watch all of your World Cup favourites battle it out, even when they play in different national club competitions.

READ MORE All the records broken during the 2026 FIFA World Cup

Qualifying rounds are already underway with the league phase starting 8 September.

Women's World Cup countdown begins

The 2026 FIFA World Cup is over, but the build-up to the 2027 Women's World Cup is underway.

Hosted by Brazil, the Matildas have already qualified for a spot in the July 2027 tournament while other qualifying matches are continuing this year.

Women's club competitions start in the US and Europe shortly after the local men's tournaments.

Many of the Matildas including Steph Catley, Kyra Cooney-Cross and Mary Fowler play in the English Women's Super League, which has its first match on 4 September.

Australian Championship

The second season of the Australian Championship is set to kick off in October, with eight foundation clubs and eight NPL premiers from across the country taking part in its second edition.

This is a tournament-style format where four groups of four teams will see the top two from each progress to a sudden-death finals series, with the Australian Championship trophy the grand prize.

READ MORE How to watch 2026 Australian Championship LIVE on SBS

The competition is not connected to the A-League Men or any of its associated clubs. The Australian Championship is the national second tier for football in Australia, with no promotion or relegation between the two at this time.

The opening match kicks off on October 16, LIVE in full on SBS and SBS On Demand, with the fixture yet to be announced.

title: WATCH: Super-sub Ferran Torres' goal wins Spain second World Cup
text:
In Brief Spain are the World Cup champions for the second time in the team's history.

Argentina have finished second after they were unable to put a score on the ledger.

Spain have seized the World Cup from the hands of defending champions Argentina after a late strike from Ferran Torres to seal a 1-0 victory in the New Jersey final.

Riding the momentum of their record-breaking fourth 2024 European Championship victory, Spain have etched their names into football history with a legacy win — taking home the trophy of all trophies for the second time, after their iconic 2010 World Cup triumph and making them the first nation in football history to hold the men's and women's World Cup titles at the same time.

The team remains the only nation ever to win the European Championship and World Cup in successive tournaments, having now done so a second time after their Euro 2024 title. They are also the first World Cup champion ever to concede only once across an entire tournament, and they are the first winner of the UEFA Nations League to capture a World title, having captured the eight-year-old competition in 2022-2023.

"We've won everything, everything," said Spain manager Luis de la Fuente through an interpreter. "And that's wonderful, with a generation of footballers, I'll say it again and again, who are an example for Spanish sport, for Spanish youth, for Spain. Together we are stronger, let there be no doubt about that."

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

For Argentina and skipper Lionel Messi — despite showing pluck and courage until the whistle — it was a somewhat disappointing end to a World Cup campaign that until this match showed they were full of surprises.

The Argentines had been dreaming of a fourth World Cup and a back-to-back trophy after winning against France at Qatar in 2022— but it was not to be.

Spain appeared to dominate for much of the game with Argentina barely getting a look in, but it was only in extra time that one of Spain's dozens of shots broke through.

In the 106th minute, well into extra time, Ferran Torres managed to land a strike into the roof of the net, just as a penalty shootout felt all but assured.

Torres was a late-game sub, taking the pitch in the 62nd minute.

"It was a goal scored by 47 million people, it wasn’t even mine or from the 26 players. It was destined to happen; it was ‌meant to be a winner," Torres said, speaking after the win.

It was 27C and sunny for the 80,000-strong crowd at New York New Jersey Stadium but it took close to full time for gameplay to heat up on the pitch.

After 49 minutes of bitsy, defensive play, the two sides were locked on 0-0 when they cleared the pitch for the half-time show. The score would not budge until well after the 90-minute mark.

As the clock wound down, chaos broke out around Spain's net — Messi taking back-to-back corners, Argentina making breathless attempt after attempt that they were unable to manoeuvre past Spain's formidable defensive line.

Spain dominated early, using their 67 per cent possession to show off their skills in the midfield and taunt Argentina, but the defending European champions only managed to convert three of those plays into attempts on goal going into the entertainment.

Around the five-minute mark, Lamine Yamal played a one-two in the box, collecting at the near post and getting a shot off that was saved by Emiliano Martinez.

Despite the early excitement, the first half wore on with few significant breaks. While Spain looked in control, neither side looked concerned.

After the first hydration break, Messi began dropping deeper and deeper into the midfield to pick up the play, only for Spain's defence to sweep him up every time.

The 39-year-old, eight-time Ballon d'Or winner Messi would have hoped for this final game to help him to at least nudge back into equal position with France's Kylian Mbappé as a top World Cup goal scorer.

Going into the match, the bowed-out Mbappé had 22 World Cup goals under his belt, compared to Messi's 21. But another wasn't to be, and with this largely rumoured to be his last time at the tournament, that is probably where his still mighty legacy will remain.

An apparent injury saw off Argentine centre-back Lisandro Martínez in the 43rd minute, to be replaced by Nicolás Otamendi.

The second half immediately marked a shift in play for both sides. When the teams returned from the not-quite-regulation 25-minute half-time break, they were both much livelier, but it wasn't enough to shift the game's play.

Some 43 seconds in, Spain's Alejandro Baena took an early chance on target — that was comfortably saved — before Argentina decided they had had enough of that and started taking more possession.

Despite more zest, the start-stop play that dominated the first half returned and Argentina remained defensive— the attacking side that came out of the gates swinging in the second half against England in the semi-final nowhere to be seen.

Spain continued to notch up attempts on goal, getting increasingly dangerous — but not dangerous enough — by attempt eight: A cross from Yamal offered Torres the chance to fling a header at speed into Martinez's waiting hands.

It offered a rare glimmer of excitement but it was not enough to change the pace as both teams headed into the tournament's last hydration break, increasingly tense and locked at nil-nil.

Off the back of the hydration break a frenzied feeling settled into play but not enough to change the score. Spain continued to make attempts on goal, but each one on target is saved without hesitation or fumble by Martínez.

Messi’s influence was almost completely negated time and time again by the well-organised and controlling Spanish midfield. Spain's midfield acted like a curtain until, in the 92nd minute — halfway through stoppage time— when Argentina made its first serious attack, only one of two it would make across the entire match.

As the game moved into extra time Argentina's side looked threadbare — down to just 10 men after Enzo Fernandez was sent off on a second yellow card; Messi the only attacker after Fernandez was sent off for an out-of-control tackle and Julian Alvarez came off in extra time.

The first goal of the game came well past the 90-minute mark, when Williams punted a defended ball into the net, only for it to be ruled out off the back of a penalty Mikel Merino incurred by standing on Nicolás Otamendi’s foot.

Nine minutes later, they nearly doubled their lead after Torres got a one-on-one with Martinez, but the offside flag was up, and it was thrown out.

Spain finished with 20 attempts, 12 on target, compared to Argentina's two off-target attempts.

Speaking after the game, Torres and his manager were candid about the game's slow evolution.

"Well, in the end, all finals are difficult, and when you have Messi on the opposing team, you always get nervous," Torres said.

"But ultimately, we've always depended on ourselves, on playing our style of football, and I think we demonstrated that once again today."

"Yes, I think the game should have been decided much earlier," de la Fuente added.

"Dibu's great saves prevented us from winning the game sooner. We could have taken a more comfortable lead. But this is a world championship final, and even with 10 men, you have to suffer at the end. But since we like to suffer, we've shown once again that we're ready for anything."

Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain were the better side but said he was proud of his side.

READ MORE All the records broken during the 2026 FIFA World Cup

"They were better, that’s the truth. But I’ll hold on to an enormous memory of what they’ve done, of what it’s worth to get this far," he said.

"We're great in victory, and we have to be great in defeat.

"Today we're showing that we know how to lose. We lost the match, and we're owning it.

"But that doesn’t mean we’ll stop remembering everything we did to get here.

Scaloni said he hadn't spoken with Messi about his future, but his tournament had been "unbelievable".

"He's 39 years old now," Scaloni said.

"I hope that everyone feels proud of him, of what he's achieved, because he's the best football player ever to set foot on a pitch. I have no doubt about this, and what he did during this World Cup was incredible. But also what he did before this."

— With additional reporting by Reuters news agency.

title: 'Impossible': Nauru community detainees say Australian funds not enough to buy food with
text:
IN BRIEF Community detainees in Nauru say they are not being given enough money by the Australian government to live on.

99 per cent of people interviewed said they don't have enough money for food.

Asylum seekers being detained within the community in Nauru are struggling to buy enough food and are being forced to choose between eating and paying for other necessities.

The difficulties are being driven by a mix of high living costs and low income support, according to a new report by the Asylum Seeker Resource Centre.

Of 78 people interviewed for the report, 99 per cent said they couldn't afford to buy enough food with their current allowance.

On top of that, 68 per cent said they rarely or never had access to fresh fruit, vegetables and meat, and 82 per cent said they didn't have easy access to free, clean drinking water.

Community detainees live on $260 a fortnight to cover all of their basic needs outside accommodation.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

The group have been sent to the tiny island nation under Australia's long-standing policy of offshore processing for asylum-seekers who arrive by boat.

Speaking anonymously, detainees consistently told the centre the allowance wasn't enough to sustain them, forcing them to decide between buying food and other necessities like clothing and internet access.

READ MORE Hundreds of former detainees to be deported to Nauru set to live among community

Many were running out of money before their next payment and were forced to reduce meals or go without food altogether.

"With food we have to compromise, if you buy vegetables you can't afford rice. If you buy meat, you can't afford fruit," one said.

"The money is not enough because everything is expensive here — rice, fish, we cannot buy any fruit here, internet for $100 per month, how is it possible to survive?" said another.

Nauru struggles with food insecurity due to its remote location and limited local agriculture, with perishable food flown in weekly and often selling out quickly or being prohibitively expensive.

With no rivers or freshwater lakes, the island also relies primarily on rainwater harvesting and desalination, meaning water shortages are common during times of low rainfall, forcing those who live there to turn to bottled water.

Asylum seekers in the community often struggle due to their lack of orientation or local knowledge as to how food systems work, as well as infrequent transportation to and from markets, the resource centre said.

As of 31 May, there were 101 people seeking asylum in Nauru, 91 of whom were in community accommodation.

The majority of those in detention in Nauru were young to middle-aged men from south and east Asia.

The group of asylum seekers are separate from a group of hundreds of former immigration detainees, known as the NZYQ cohort, sent to Nauru from Australia after the High Court in 2024 ruled indefinite detention to be unlawful.

title: Australia has an unlikely tourist attraction — and fans are spending thousands to reach it
text:
Vincent Simmons has never left the United States before. But in a few days, he'll board a 15-hour flight to Melbourne. Not for the beaches, the coffee or the art — but for a cinema.

Splitting the cost across a few credit cards, the 35-year-old Los Angeles projectionist has put close to $4,000 into flights and accommodation for the three-week trip, all to watch The Odyssey — the latest film from Oscar-winning director Christopher Nolan, released in Australia on 16 July — at Melbourne's IMAX.

He'll see it there twice — on top of a screening back home in LA just before he flies out.

"I've heard about the IMAX in Melbourne for years — I've probably watched every YouTube video that shows even a sliver of it," Simmons tells SBS News. "I told myself I'd go one day."

"70mm IMAX offers hands down the greatest sensory engrossment you could possibly have when watching a film," he explains.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

"It's the closest sense of lifelike fidelity — a transcendent film format that everyone should experience at least once in their life. But to be fair, if they experience it once, they will want to experience it again."

Vincent Simmons has never left the United States before. But he's about to travel all the way to Melbourne to see a movie. Source: Supplied / Vincent Simmons

He calls the trip his own odyssey.

"I was brave enough to undertake my own odyssey in travelling to Melbourne," he says.

"Hopefully it won't be as burdensome as Odysseus' journey!"

Not any old cinema

Melbourne's IMAX isn't just any old cinema.

It's one of only 41 in the world equipped to project true IMAX 70mm film, the largest film stock in existence — so large that the camera has to be reloaded every three minutes during a shoot.

At 32 metres wide and 23 metres tall, and with an aspect ratio of 1.43:1, the Melbourne IMAX's screen is also the biggest of its kind anywhere.

Jeremy Fee says the Melbourne IMAX is the only place in the Southern Hemisphere film-lovers can see The Odyssey as Christopher Nolan intended. Source: Supplied / Eugene Hyland

"Because we're only one of 41 cinemas in the entire world that can screen this film on IMAX 70mm on our large 1.43:1 aspect ratio screen, it's a place that customers seek out," Jeremy Fee, general manager at IMAX Melbourne, tells SBS News.

"This is the only place in Australia, in the southern hemisphere, that you can see it in the way that Christopher Nolan intended."

Just seven of those theatres are outside of North America.

The film print itself is a "massive beast", Fee says.

Laid out end to end, a single print of The Odyssey stretches more than 17km.

Only five people in Australia are qualified to handle the 70mm film reels that Odysseus will be projected from at IMAX Melbourne. Source: Supplied / Eugene Hyland

Its reels run on platters so large they can hold three hours of runtime — just eight minutes more than The Odyssey's own two hours and 52 minutes.

Each session requires constant quality assurance checks to keep the film running true — and just five people in Australia are currently qualified to do it.

"It's a perishable skill," he says. "It was [a dying craft] … I think it's a growing art form now."

While a renewed appreciation for the craft may have averted — or at least delayed — its demise, the current scarcity of those accredited to carry it out is exactly what's pulling people across oceans to watch a movie in Melbourne.

An odyssey in itself

At Melbourne's IMAX opening weekend for The Odyssey, it's a similar story up and down the queue: people have travelled a long way to be here.

Kaivalya, a 28-year-old PhD student and Nolan-fanatic, is in the middle of relocating to Melbourne from Maharashtra, India — but pushed his flights forward by a month so he could catch The Odyssey early.

It's not the first time cinema has dictated his travel plans; in 2014, he travelled 400km from his hometown in India to see Nolan's Interstellar on the nearest IMAX screen he could find.

Now, Melbourne's IMAX is about to become his local.

"I intend to watch many movies in this theatre in the coming months," he says. "It will be my go-to destination for movies for the next year."

Kaivalya moved to Australia an entire month earlier than originally planned so he could see The Odyssey during its opening weekend. Source: SBS News

His eyes light up when he talks about why film wins out over digital.

"This is a much more immersive experience because the resolution is much higher," he says. "The colours feel warmer. I love watching the grain in the movie — it gives it life."

His friends back home are also chasing a similar experience.

"My friends in India are travelling internationally — some to China, some to the US. The nearest IMAX 70mm theatre to India is in Thailand, so many people are travelling there and applying for visas," he says.

"It's like a concert."

Mitchell and Dimeth, both 22, didn't travel as far — a weekend trip down from Brisbane — but they've built an entire ritual around seeing Nolan's films at the Melbourne cinema.

"We came last time to see Oppenheimer," Mitchell tells SBS News. "It's kind of our thing — for Nolan, we'll come and watch it here."

He was online the moment general tickets dropped last month: "I waited an hour to make sure we got good tickets," he says.

Their closest IMAX, on the Gold Coast, is digital and smaller.

Friends and hardcore Nolan fans Mitchell and Dimeth have travelled from Brisbane to Melbourne multiple times to watch the renowned director's films. Source: SBS News

Dimeth says their closest IMAX on the Gold Coast, which is smaller and runs digitally, doesn't compare to the scale and theatre of Melbourne.

"This level of immersion isn't something you get in many other cinemas," he says.

Mitchell reckons the hype has only made the tickets more valuable.

"I think people are jealous that we get to see it," he says. "Someone I work with was trying to buy a ticket off me."

A tourist attraction

Just seven weeks of sessions have been released so far, with months of screenings still to come. But records are expected to be broken.

"We're up to 52,500 people who have bought tickets to The Odyssey so far in this cinema," Fee says, adding that each session can only seat 450 people.

Box office for Odyssey at the Melbourne IMAX is already well past $2 million, rapidly closing in on the cinema's all-time record of $2.78 million set by another Nolan film, Oppenheimer. It's currently the 7th highest-grossing film of all time in the theatre — with no signs of slowing down.

"We're so close to already knocking that one over," Fee says.

"We've got people coming from Germany, Turkey, New Zealand, India, Sri Lanka … I've never seen the amount of hype for a release as I have for this one."

READ MORE This film starring Rose Byrne airs the unspeakable parts of motherhood

But there are still challenges — and cinemas like Melbourne's can't simply be replicated elsewhere.

The film projection system was pulled from the cinema in 2015 and sat dormant for two years before being reinstalled in 2017 at Nolan's request for his film Dunkirk.

"You have to invest a lot of money," Fee says.

"For Oppenheimer, there were 30 film projector sites for IMAX. They managed to find another 11 projectors for The Odyssey to get it up to 41. I don't know if there's any more out there, and they're not building any more."

"So it's a really lucky and fortuitous thing that we still have our film projection system — and that those 40 other theatres across the globe have held onto theirs as well."

Fee expects the same scenes to play out again in December, when Denis Villeneuve's Dune: Part Three — also shot substantially on IMAX 70mm — opens on 17 December.

For now, though, Melbourne has an unlikely tourist attraction on its hands.

"It's a tourism boom for Melbourne, for us to be able to have this here," he says. "The fact that people are building their holidays and their trips around coming to Melbourne is super exciting."

"It's changed cinema into this event."

title: Spain beat Argentina 1-0 to win World Cup final — as it happened
text:
Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain were the better side but said he was proud of his side.

Argentine coach Lionel Scaloni said Spain were the better side but he was proud of his men. Source: Getty / Alex Livesey

"They were better, that’s the truth. But I’ll hold on to an enormous memory of what they’ve done, of what it’s worth to get this far," he said.

"We're great in victory, and we have to be great in defeat.

"Today we're showing that we know how to lose. We lost the match, and we're owning it.

"But that doesn’t mean we’ll stop remembering everything we did to get here.

Scaloni said he hadn't spoken with Messi about his future, but his tournament had been "unbelievable".

"He's 39 years old now," Scaloni said.

"It was crystal clear for me that he was going to play up until he decided not to.

"I hope that everyone feels proud of him, of what he's achieved, because he's the best football player ever to set foot on a pitch. I have no doubt about this, and what he did during this World Cup was incredible. But also what he did before this."

— Miles Proust

title: Hanson tells UK conservative conference audience to 'not apologise for being white'
text:
In brief Hanson told a UK crowd that Australia shouldn't introduce cultures "incompatible" with the local way of life.

Australian politicians have criticised Hanson's recent comments as un-Australian and unfair.

Don't apologise for being white, Pauline Hanson has said at a conservative conference in London amid a backdrop of growing backlash over her comments on migration and transgender rights.

Speaking at the Conservative Political Action Conference (CPAC) on Saturday morning UK time, the One Nation leader told a sparse crowd that Australia had been "flooded" with migrants and gone "completely woke" over transgender rights.

Her speech capped a high-profile European trip that included a podcast interview with English anti-immigration activist and convicted criminal Tommy Robinson and an appearance at the Dolce & Gabbana fashion show in Italy with billionaire One Nation donor Gina Rinehart.

"I'm getting feedback from Australia, from the media, saying, 'How dare I associate with Tommy Robinson, how dare I speak to him,'" Hanson told a conference room that, judging by footage posted online, looked less than one-third full.

"Do not apologise for being white. I'm sick of hearing about white privilege."

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Hanson also said Australia was "too stupid" to get its laws around discrimination against transgender people right and praised a UK court decision that ruled the legal definition of a woman depended on their biological sex.

"There's going to be so much psychological damage that's going to be done to our future generations," she said.

Hanson had already come under fire in Australia earlier in the week for her interview with Robinson, in which she pointed to the scrapping of the White Australia policy as the root cause of migration issues in the country.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese addressed those comments on Saturday, saying it was important to remember that the racially discriminatory immigration policy was dismantled by both sides of politics and challenged the Opposition leader to rule out any future political deals with One Nation.

Hanson later released a video on social media saying allegations that she wanted to reintroduce the White Australia policy were false. "Never, ever have I advocated that, and I don't believe in that," she said.

"Yes, I've been strong on immigration. Bringing the right people into the country in a measured way and people who want to assimilate and be Australians."

'It's farcical'

Other speakers at the London CPAC event included Nationals senator Bridget McKenzie, Reform UK leader Nigel Farage and short-lived former Tory prime minister Liz Truss.

After an earlier meeting with Farage fell over due to his resignation from the UK parliament to re-contest his seat, Hanson and the Reform leader met briefly on the sidelines of the conference.

Hanson also told the conference that Australia had brought in too many immigrants, and this was putting too much pressure on the housing system.

She also said migrants should want to be Australian and not introduce cultures that were "incompatible" with the Australian way of life.

"Do I want Sharia Law? No way in the whole wide world," she said.

"Do I want child marriages? No."

"Do I want the burqa seen on our streets. No."

Gamel Kheir from the Lebanese Muslim Association — who spoke to SBS News on Saturday about similar comments Hanson made during her Tommy Robinson interview — challenged the One Nation leader to provide evidence of such claims.

"If she's going to throw that sort of slur that we somehow practice Sharia law, where is the evidence for that?" he said.

"I have yet to meet anyone Muslim or non-Muslim who advocates for their own law in contrast and in competition with the Australian law. It's farcical."

Backlash grows

Asked about Hanson's remarks, Liberal moderate and frontbencher Andrew Bragg said Hanson was going out of her way to make life harder for minorities.

"The idea that there's a spectre of transgender people taking over Australia, I just think is insane," he told ABC's Insiders program on Sunday.

However, he refused to be drawn on whether the Coalition should rule out a potential preference deal with One Nation at the next federal election.

Instead, he extended his criticism to the government, insisting that picking between Labor and One Nation would be difficult as both were "terrible".

In early June, polling emerged that suggested One Nation was Australia's most popular political party. While more recent surveys have indicated a drop in primary support for One Nation, the party remains ahead of the Coalition.

Deputy Liberal leader Jane Hume told Sky News on Sunday that Hanson's comments about the White Australia policy were "unnecessary", "divisive", "inflammatory" and "totally un-Australian".

She said that, in her discussions with Australians, no-one had brought up the racially discriminatory immigration scheme or the idea of banning the burqa.

NSW Premier Chris Minns added that it was "blatantly unfair" and untrue to blame non-white migrants for any problems that existed in Australia, adding that any attempt to return to past discriminatory policies would undermine the nation's standing.

"That would make Australia a pariah nation, probably one of the only in the world that had that kind of race-based immigration policies," he said.

title: Sydney family embrace divided loyalties ahead of Spain-Argentina World Cup showdown
text:
IN BRIEF The World Cup final between Spain and Argentina kicks off at 5am Monday morning (AEST).

While Spain have only won the cup once before, Argentina are vying for their fourth victory.

Whatever the result in the FIFA World Cup final, it will be a win-win for Sydney couple Sandra Hurtado and Fernando Travasso.

On Monday morning (AEST), Spaniard Hurtado will be wearing a La Roja jersey while her partner, Travasso, will be backing his native Argentina as the two sides meet at New York New Jersey Stadium.

Their three children — twins Sofia and Carla, 10, and Marco, 11 — have grown up with both cultures. While that makes for some divided loyalties in the household, their parents stress that there's no bad blood.

"For me, especially, it feels like the cup is already home because whoever wins doesn't matter for us," Hurtado told SBS News.

"Whoever wins is a win for us ... I'm gonna celebrate anyway."

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Her daughter Sofia also doesn't mind which way the results go.

"I just want both to win, and even if one of them loses, I think they can improve next time and get better," she said.

Despite the Argentine jerseys, siblings Carla, Sofia and Marco (left to right) say they don't mind which team wins. Source: SBS News

"As both of my siblings said, it's very hard to choose."

A 'great excuse to be even more connected'

Hurtado and Travasso arrived in Australia in 2008 and have spent almost two decades building a life together while keeping their Spanish and Argentine traditions alive.

Travasso said the World Cup had become another way to stay connected to family on the other side of the world.

"I think it's a great excuse to be even more connected," he said.

"We've been heavily involved in the community [and] integrated into Australian society, but we still manage to keep our identity and [pass on] the values and history of each of our cultures.

"Every day we have conversations with our relatives. When the World Cup comes ... and we have both countries together ... we're going to be using WhatsApp ... at the same time."

The final also promises some friendly rivalry at home.

"Once we sit down there with our different T-shirts and all the things, I still think that would be a nice competition, it would be a little bit spicy, and we are going to have good fun," Travasso said.

Growing up in Spain, Hurtado said football was deeply embedded in the culture.

"In Spain, for us, football is the same as AFL [is for Australia]," she said.

READ MORE Argentina are one win from glory — and fans are turning to lucky rituals

"Spain has amazing football players, and we have amazing teams that are very good [in the European tournaments]."

She said that, before winning the World Cup in 2010, Spain had "never made it too far".

"Hopefully now, we're gonna win."

'It's part of the identity'

While Spain are chasing their second title, Argentina have a chance for their fourth.

It would also be a second consecutive championship for Messi's side after their 2022 win — a feat that would make it the first nation to go back-to-back since Brazil in 1962.

Like Spain, football is more than just a sport in Argentina.

"It's part of the identity," Travasso said.

"Through different cities in Argentina, you can find big murals or big paintings on the wall with [Lionel] Messi.

"A few memories that I have when I was little ... were a TV, white and black, with [Diego] Maradona playing."

Hurtado said she hopes her children take away something more important than the result.

"I think Argentinians are the same [as Spanish]," she said.

"Both Argentina and Spain have demonstrated that they are fighters.

"In life it's not about one person, it's about a team. When someone wins, it's not just the person who scores the goal; it's everybody working together towards the same goal.

"That's what we're trying to [teach our children]."

title: Could Alzheimer’s be predicted years before symptoms? A test offers new clues
text:
IN BRIEF The so-called p-tau217 test had previously been used only on those with symptoms.

Some experts are already calling it a "game changer".

What if a simple test could reveal your risk of Alzheimer's years before memory problems start?

A new study on a specific blood test shows this might be possible.

The test looks for what doctors call 'p-tau217' and can alert them to the disease five to 10 years before a healthy person develops symptoms.

That information could be reassuring or terrifying, but for now, it's a potential tool to speed drug development by helping to identify and enrol high-risk people into studies of possible Alzheimer's treatments or preventive strategies.

The scientists behind the study, Reisa Sperling from the Mass General Brigham Neuroscience Institute in the US, said the so-called p-tau217 test had previously been used only on those with symptoms.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

"Right now it's really being utilised in people who already have symptoms of Alzheimer's disease and are thought to have cognitive impairment due to Alzheimer's disease," she said.

"In this study, we are looking at people who do not yet have symptoms of Alzheimer's disease."

How does the Alzheimer's test work?

The results, presented at the Alzheimer's Association International Conference in London, showed similar patterns in those at risk of developing Alzheimer’s.

It found that symptom-free older adults who harboured very high levels of p-tau217 in the blood test had a 38 per cent risk of developing cognitive impairment over five years. That risk grew to 78 per cent by 10 years.

"We also found that people who have a very low level of this blood test, p-tau217, were very unlikely to progress over time and were about the same level as people in general as they age over a 10-year period might be at risk for developing impairment," Sperling said

READ MORE My mum is living her worst fear; I just found out I have the same gene

She said the blood test data offers some new clues. While different intermediate levels of p-tau217 signalled progressive risk, only the very highest level seemed to correlate with other evidence about that tipping point.

Researchers analysed data from 2,684 healthy older adults enrolled in long-running Alzheimer's studies since 2004, all of whom took the test when they joined. By last year, 478 of them had developed cognitive impairment.

A 'game changer'

The test can reveal a person's risk years in advance, information that may be reassuring for some and distressing for others, but it does not offer a cure.

"At this point, it wouldn't change what I would tell someone to do. I'd still tell them to eat well, sleep well, exercise a lot and stay engaged. But that's going to change dramatically if the current prevention trials show clinical benefit," Sperling said.

Already, large clinical trials are testing whether certain drugs could prevent or at least delay the disease.

If any of those works, doctors should have an easy way to tell who should try them — and the blood test can be exactly what they need.

That is why Jessica Langbaum from the Banner Alzheimer’s Institute based in the US, explained the development as a "game changer" for neary 10 people who are diagnosed with Alzheimer's every year.

"This is really important for people, particularly if we get to a time when there's one of the treatments that are being tested for Alzheimer's prevention therapies," she said.

"If one of those treatments read out having a blood test that can accurately determine who to test and say, yes, this person might be eligible for treatment, I think that's a real game changer."

— With additional reporting by the Associated Press
