title: Watch: England beat France 6-4 after holding off stunning second-half fightback
text:
England have held off a remarkable French comeback to win the World Cup third-place playoff 6-4, securing their best finish at the tournament in 60 years.

England led 4-0 at half-time after a stunning opening 45 minutes that included a Bukayo Saka double and the Three Lions' second-fastest goal in World Cup history by Declan Rice.

But France mounted an extraordinary second-half fightback at Miami Stadium, scoring three goals in 18 minutes to cut the deficit to one.

Kylian Mbappé scored twice, becoming the highest goalscorer in World Cup history and moving ahead of Argentina's Lionel Messi in the Golden Boot race, a trophy awarded to the tournament's highest scorer.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Both sides made sweeping changes to the line-ups that lost to Argentina and Spain in the semi-finals, with England coach Thomas Tuchel's decision to rest captain Harry Kane and Jude Bellingham paying off as his fringe players seized their opportunity in the opening half.

Stand-in captain Rice opened the scoring after just three minutes, intercepting a sloppy pass from Désiré Doué before firing home from outside the box. It was England's second-fastest goal in World Cup history.

Ezri Konsa doubled England's lead with a glancing header from a corner delivered by Rice in the 18th minute.

Saka, who had a goal ruled out for offside in the 12th minute, struck twice before the break. He netted from close range in the 37th minute before adding his second in first-half stoppage time to give England a stunning 4-0 lead at the interval.

France finally found a response three minutes into the second half when star forward Mbappé finished from close range.

Bradley Barcola then reduced the deficit to two in the 54th minute, hammering the ball past England goalkeeper Dean Henderson, who started in place of Jordan Pickford.

Mbappé's second goal in the 66th minute made him the outright leading scorer in World Cup history with 22 goals and moved him ahead of Messi in the Golden Boot standings, with 10 goals this tournament.

Messi, who is second with eight goals, will have the chance to reclaim the lead in Monday morning's final against Spain.

England restored their two-goal cushion when Saka completed his hat-trick with an 87th-minute penalty after Djed Spence was fouled in the box.

But France struck again in the sixth minute of stoppage time through Ousmane Dembélé to make it 5-4.

Jude Bellingham then sealed the result moments later with another goal, as England prevailed in a 10-goal thriller.

It was the highest-scoring match in a third-place playoff in tournament history, surpassing France's 6-3 victory over West Germany at the 1958 tournament.

England's best finish since 1966 title

The third-place result is England's best finish at a World Cup since their first and only title win in 1966. They have lost both of their previous third-place playoffs — against Italy in 1990 and Belgium in 2018.

It was a disappointing end for French coach Didier Deschamps, whose final match came after 14 years in charge.

His tenure has included his side winning a World Cup title in 2018, a runner-up finish in 2022 and three consecutive semi-final appearances.

If Messi, 39, scores twice against Spain, he will reclaim the record as the World Cup's all-time leading goalscorer. He currently has 21 goals, one behind Mbappé.

But while this is likely to be Messi's final World Cup, Mbappé, 27, is expected to have more opportunities to add to his tally and could reclaim the record in future tournaments.

Speaking after the loss, Mbappé said: "Messi is going to score [against Spain] that's for sure. I'm just trying to help my team win. When you score a lot of goals at the World Cup, it puts you in a certain category."

"I would have preferred not to be the all-time top scorer and to be playing tomorrow's match. It's good in terms of legacy, but today it's not the first thing on my mind."

The match was the 33rd meeting between England and France, with the last fixture at the 2022 World Cup quarter-final, which France won 2-1.

title: NSW gold-antimony plant springs to life as Larvotto nears production
text:
Advertisement

Sponsored BusinessCompaniesBulls N' Bears NSW gold-antimony plant springs to life as Larvotto nears production Brought to you by BULLS N’ BEARS Doug Bright July 21, 2026 — 12:58pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

In a red-letter milestone for the company, Larvotto Resources has kicked off staged commissioning at its Hillgrove antimony-gold mine in New South Wales, marking the next step in a carefully sequenced restart that began underground at its nearby Metz mine. Larvotto’s underground contractor PYBAR has been pushing forward with the Metz workings and rehabilitating old drives since mining restarted, with first development ore delivered to surface stockpiles in May and ore stockpiles continuing to build since then. Larvotto Resources’ secondary crusher with the fine ore storage behind, at the company’s Hillgrove antimony-gold operation in NSW. That ore has now begun moving from stockpiles to the run-of-mine pad, before being fed into the primary jaw crusher, closed-circuit secondary crusher and screening circuit. From there, the material is then conveyed to the fine ore bin. The company says the crushing, screening and fine ore storage section has been successfully commissioned as the first stage of the plant restart. The resulting ore is now sitting in storage ready to feed the milling circuit during the upcoming phase of wet-end commissioning.

Advertisement

‘Hillgrove will become Australia’s largest antimony producer, and with tungsten potential growing.’ Larvotto Resources managing director Ron Heeks Once the fine ore bin is full, Larvotto plans to run the crushing circuit on waste for a limited period to generate crushed rock for various engineering requirements, allowing more time to bed the crushing system down. It will also give operators familiarisation time on the circuit. EPCM contractor Interquip, meanwhile, is completing its work on the communications, electrical, instrumentation and control systems at the wet end of the plant before that phase is commissioned. Hillgrove is forecast to become Australia’s second and largest producing antimony mine, with Larvotto pointing to the planned production of around 4900 tonnes of antimony -almost seven per cent of global production - and more than 40,000 ounces of gold a year across the current seven-year mine life. The company says its tungsten testwork is continuing, with multiple zones of tungsten mineralisation identified and unrecovered tungsten from previous operations sitting in the old tailings dam, all pointing to the potential for a third critical minerals string to Hillgrove’s bow.

Advertisement

Larvotto Resources managing director Ron Heeks said: “The commencement of commissioning is a defining milestone for Larvotto and reflects the significant progress made over the past twelve months since the plant refurbishment started. To reach this point is a tremendous achievement by our operations team and Interquip, the build contractors, and demonstrates the quality of execution, especially given the unique challenges that were, and continue to be, created by the Hormuz conflict on equipment deliveries.” Larvotto’s marketing pathways are also falling into place. Last month, the company locked in a binding seven-year offtake deal with Glencore International for its Hillgrove gold concentrate, complementing its previously announced antimony concentrate agreement with Wogen Resources. The Glencore agreement is expected to cover 15,000 dry metric tonnes of gold concentrate a year. Larvotto is also pushing ahead with its Modification 5 application process with the NSW government, seeking approval to lift its processing capacity from 250,000 to 500,000 tonnes a year, while also enabling dry-stack tailings and other environmental improvements. The company says responses to questions from the NSW Department of Planning, Housing and Infrastructure and other stakeholders are being finalised and are expected to be submitted by the end of July. Larvotto does not view the matters raised as material issues, instead describing them as being part of the appropriate level of review expected for a project of Hillgrove’s scale and strategic importance. With ore already passing through the front end of the plant, offtake partners signed up for its key concentrate products and the early stages of its process plant commissioning underway, Hillgrove is steadily working through its transition from development to a producing mine.

Advertisement

For Larvotto, the next few weeks will be all about transforming a historic mining field into a modern critical minerals operation, with antimony and gold leading the production charge and the prospect of a valuable tungsten by-product adding another layer of upside. title: No preference deal yet, but Hanson vows to put Labor last in Secret Harbour byelection
text:
Advertisement

NationalWAOne Nation No preference deal yet, but Hanson vows to put Labor last in Secret Harbour byelection Michael Philipps July 21, 2026 — 12:57pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

One Nation leader Pauline Hanson says a preference deal is yet to be struck with WA’s Liberal party ahead of the upcoming Secret Harbour byelection, but vowed to put both the Greens and Labor last. Speaking after her appearance at a business breakfast hosted by The West Australian, the federal senator said she hoped to work with the Liberals and Nationals to take the seat from Labor. One Nation leader Pauline Hanson campaigning with candidate Luke Herdegen in Secret Harbour on Monday. “This will be a test case, the same as when we won seven seats in the South Australian parliament,” Hanson said. “A deal has not been struck, but we will be putting Labor last and the Greens second last.”

Advertisement

The Queensland senator also dubbed Gina Rinehart “a wonderful West Australian” while defending the billionaire mining heiress’ support for One Nation. Hanson has received criticism from opposing political parties and the media after Rinehart provided significant support for the Queenslander, including an airplane, and as photos emerged of the pair holidaying in Europe. “Look at those taxes, royalties, and jobs that she provides the state,” Hanson told the audience after the Hancock Prospecting boss was brought up by West Australian editor Chris Dore at the breakfast event. “It didn’t cost the taxpayer one cent for my trip overseas. Not one cent.

Advertisement

“You think that she’s come with a silver spoon. She didn’t. “She had to borrow everything and was flat out in the very start to pay the bills. She’s built up that company. “A very smart, clever woman. And even now, to this day, who supports the [Royal] Flying Doctor Service. Who supports the Olympic swimmers and the lobbyists. “She’s a very proud, patriotic Australian, and she sees the same in me.” Hanson also used her appearance to highlight the important role WA played in the nation’s economy while also addressing the continued housing crisis in the state.

Advertisement

She said WA was being held back by the federal government. Related Article Political leadership Why these Brisbane Islamic school students spoke to Pauline Hanson “Western Australia helps to pay this nation’s bills. Your royalties fund the services here,” she said. “Your taxes fund them everywhere else. Western Australia is home to roughly one in nine Australians, but generates one in $6 of national economic output. “Western Australia generates almost one in $5 of national business investment. It generates almost half of the country’s exported goods.

Advertisement

“But your businesses are being held back by this government.” Hanson pointed to Perth’s rental vacancy rate of 0.6 per cent – the tightest capital city in the country after Darwin – and blamed the federal government’s migration policy for the increase. “Families with nowhere to live in the richest state in the nation. And let me tell you why there’s nowhere to live, it’s not a mystery,” she said. “Last year, this country’s population grew by more than 400,000 people. In the same year, we finished about 173,000 homes, nearly 2½ people for every new home, and it’s not slowing down. “In just four years, this government will have added almost 1.6 million people through net overseas migration. Nearly one million arrived in 2022 and 2023 alone.

Advertisement

“This government is running the biggest migration program in our history, and building nowhere near the homes to match it. Then it acts surprised when your kids can’t afford a house and services are overwhelmed. “That’s not compassion. That’s negligence.” More to come.

title: Double blow for Rabbitohs after Murray, Mitchell injury prognoses revealed
text:
South Sydney superstar Latrell Mitchell may not play again this year, in a second blow to the Rabbitohs’ finals chances after captain Cameron Murray succumbed to an ankle injury.

Murray suffered the injury in the loss to Canberra on Saturday night and is expected to be sidelined for up to a month. The loss of the NSW forward is set to be compounded by the news that Mitchell is struggling to return to the field due to his own injury issues.

Cameron Murray and Latrell Mitchell, Souths’ highest-paid players, are due for more time on the sidelines.

Mitchell’s scheduled return from a back injury last month was aborted after he tore his calf in training. It was hoped Mitchell could be fit for the round-23 clash against Parramatta on August 8, but there is no guarantee he will play again this year.

The loss of the star duo is a huge blow for the Rabbitohs, who are in seventh position on the ladder and fighting for one of the final spots in the top eight.

title: Manhattan jags 20g/t gold at new undrilled Canadian prospect
text:
Advertisement

Sponsored BusinessCompaniesBulls N' Bears Manhattan jags 20g/t gold at new undrilled Canadian prospect Brought to you by BULLS N’ BEARS Murray Ward July 21, 2026 — 12:45pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Manhattan Gold Corporation has unearthed a swag of high-grade gold results from first-pass sampling at its newly acquired and never-before-drilled Vanquish prospect in Nunavut, Canada, hitting a hefty 20 grams per tonne (g/t) gold in testing. The results from a quickfire channel and grab sampling program at the prospect within the company’s expansive Hook Lake project included impressive channel assays of 18.55g/t gold over 0.3 metres and 15.45g/t gold over 0.4m. Manhattan Gold’s Hook Lake project area in Nunavut, Canada. The new work at Vanquish, which sits 80 kilometres west of Manhattan’s flagship Jaws target, has now identified gold mineralisation over at least 131 metres of strike, significantly expanding a footprint previously defined by historic surface trenching that returned 5.75g/t gold over 1.5m. Manhattan only recently acquired the prospect in April after a review of historic data identified the biggest gold-arsenic till anomaly within the Kaminak greenstone belt, which forms part of the broader Rankin Ennadai greenstone belt.

Advertisement

‘Drilling at Jaws is advancing,samples from the initial holes are being analysed and first assay results are expected shortly.’ Manhattan Gold Corporation tecnical advisor Eric Sondergaard Despite the compelling geochemical smoke, the target has never been kissed by the drill bit. The company says the reconnaissance sampling at Vanquish was completed during the mobilisation of the drill rig and supporting equipment to Jaws. Vanquish is one of 12 prospects within Manhattan’s 665-square-kilometre Hook Lake project, which sits in the underexplored but highly prospective Rankin-Ennadai greenstone belt. The belt is also home to mining giant Agnico Eagle’s 6.7-million-ounce Meliadine operation, 225 kilometres to the northeast and one of Canada’s largest gold mines. The company says this underlines the world-class gold endowment of the region and suggests it has the potential for further significant deposits. Manhattan Gold Corporation technical advisor Eric Sondergaard said: “The early results from Vanquish are particularly exciting. This is a newly acquired, never- drilled prospect where a limited first-pass program has already delivered grades of up to 20g/t gold and confirmed mineralisation across 131 metres of strike.”

Advertisement

Manhattan’s current work is focused on a maiden reverse circulation program at its flagship Jaws target, where drilling in the 1980’s outlined a historical foreign resource estimate of 3.4 million tonnes grading 2.38g/t gold for a contained 285,000 ounces. The company is quick to say that the estimate is not compliant with the modern JORC code and it is now eagerly awaiting the first batch of assays from its Jaws drilling program to firm up the numbers for conversion into a JORC-compliant inventory. First results are expected in the coming weeks. Beyond Jaws and Vanquish, the Hook Lake project hosts a grab-bag of other compelling targets. These include the never-drilled Quantum and Lotus prospects, which have returned surface gold samples of 16.75g/t and 8.01g/t respectively. Manhattan’s other key target is Spectre, a high-grade polymetallic Volcanogenic Massive Sulphide (VMS) system where drilling in the 1970’s hit a mouthwatering 10.51m at 2.91 per cent copper, 6.7 per cent zinc and 95.67g/t silver. The company plans to integrate the new high-grade gold results with recently acquired magnetic and LiDAR data to refine the structural model and prioritise drill targets at Vanquish.

Advertisement

With the drill rods turning at its flagship Jaws prospect and a brand new, high-grade discovery at Vanquish screaming to be tested, Manhattan appears to be systematically building a serious inventory of gold targets. Punters will likely be watching to see if the drill bit at Jaws can deliver what the surface rocks at Vanquish are already suggesting. Is your ASX-listed company doing something interesting? title: Shallow gold hits put Pacgold on fast track to SA mine restart
text:
Advertisement

Sponsored BusinessCompaniesBulls N' Bears Shallow gold hits put Pacgold on fast track to SA mine restart Brought to you by BULLS N’ BEARS Penny Taylor July 21, 2026 — 12:31pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Pacgold has unearthed significant shallow oxide gold on the edge of the historical Vertigo open pit at its wholly owned White Dam project in South Australia, potentially putting the mine’s restart on the fast track. The recently completed reverse circulation drilling began with an initial 178-hole campaign before a successful first phase prompted a further 102-hole infill and extension program. The latest round was designed to upgrade much of Vertigo’s inferred resource into the higher-confidence indicated category, strengthening pit design, reserve estimation and mine planning. Aerial view showing the Vertigo deposit (bottom left), the Hannaford pit and existing heap-leach pad (upper centre), and the operating plant and SART facility (upper right) at Pacgold’s White Dam gold project in South Australia. The program delivered some impressive shallow hits, with highlights including 4 metres grading 3.1 grams per tonne (g/t) gold from surface, including a 1m slice grading 7.2g/t gold. Other notable results delivered 6m grading 2.2g/t gold from surface, 11m grading 1.2g/t gold also from surface, 17m grading 0.9g/t gold from 6m and 13m grading 1.3g/t gold from a depth of 14m.

Advertisement

‘The development from ore discovered north and east of the pit will drastically reduce pre-strip costs and bring forward gold production.’ Pacgold managing director Matthew Boyes Beyond the headline intersections, the final assays also confirmed shallow northern and western extensions to the Vertigo deposit outside the existing resource model, pointing to further resource growth. The results will now be incorporated into an updated resource estimate for Vertigo, currently being prepared by external consultants and slated for delivery early in the September quarter. Prior to the update, the White Dam project hosted a global resource of 4.6 million tonnes grading 0.7g/t gold for 102,000 ounces across the Vertigo, Hannaford and White Dam North deposits. Vertigo is the first of White Dam’s four deposits targeted for restart, putting it at the front of the queue to bring White Dam back into production. Management believes the shallow oxide mineralisation will improve both the economics and timing of bringing Vertigo back into production.

Advertisement

Pacgold managing director Matthew Boyes said: “These excellent shallow oxide intervals represent a very material win for Pacgold in respect to fast-tracking the restart of the Vertigo mine and getting shallow low-cost oxide dirt onto the old pad while extending the ore leaching capacity with the new pad expansion.” The company is already pushing forward with a heap-leach pad expansion, with engineering nearing completion ahead of construction tenders. The bigger plant will allow for additional oxide ore to be processed as operations ramp up. Since the mineralisation is shallow, oxidised and beside an existing pit, Pacgold believes it’s ideally suited for low-cost heap-leach processing, where a cyanide solution recovers gold without the expense of conventional milling. That winning combination could slash pre-strip waste removal, reduce working capital and bring forward the first new ounces of gold. Management says the focus will shortly shift to pit optimisation, mine design and cashflow modelling once the new resource model is complete. The rig has now moved on from Vertigo, with drilling already underway at White Dam North ahead of a spell at the nearby Rolling and Hannaford deposits. All three are expected to provide additional resource growth and future ore sources for the planned restart. The company is also preparing regional exploration drilling at the Wadnaminga, Wilkins, Green and Gold and Mary Mine prospects later this quarter as it hunts for additional ore sources within easy trucking distance of White Dam.

Advertisement

For a brownfield restart project such as White Dam, the gold’s proximity to the plant matters far more than the grades returned. Between 2010 and 2018, White Dam produced 180,000 ounces. It already has established open pits, a heap-leach facility, a processing plant and all the supporting infrastructure a new miner could want. Much of the operation was paid for by previous owners, allowing Pacgold to bring the project back into production far faster and cheaper than building a mine from scratch. The company’s efforts to restart mining in South Australia seem well-timed. While gold prices may have pulled back to US$4000 (A$5700) per ounce from their historic highs of more than US$5500 (A$7800) per ounce earlier in the year, major institutions remain bullish. J.P. Morgan has forecast a rebound to an average of A$6,000 per ounce in the final quarter of this year. Finding a source of low-cost, quick-to-mine ore could leave White Dam well placed if bullion regains momentum later this year. Against that backdrop, the White Dam story isn’t about chasing the next big discovery. It’s about systematically unlocking more ounces from an established mining operation and turning yesterday’s infrastructure into tomorrow’s cash flow. The latest drilling has strengthened the investment case for Vertigo to become the first White Dam deposit back in production, while longer term, Pacgold sees White Dam evolving into a regional processing hub. The next catalyst will be the updated resource model and mine plan, which could provide the blueprint for breathing new life into one of South Australia’s proven gold-producing operations. Is your ASX-listed company doing something interesting? Contact: mattbirney@bullsnbears.com.au

title: Jobs to go at Nine’s metro mastheads in restructure to drive digital push
text:
Advertisement

Breaking BusinessCompaniesMedia & marketing Jobs to go at Nine’s metro mastheads in restructure to drive digital push Calum Jaspan July 21, 2026 — 12:24pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Nine’s publishing division, which includes this masthead, will make dozens of roles redundant as part of a restructure to sharpen its focus on digital news amid a tough advertising market and rapid technological disruption in the media industry. About 30 voluntary redundancies will be initially offered to staff at The Sydney Morning Herald, The Age, WAToday and Brisbane Times as the division pushes for more digital subscribers, with staff in the division’s print operations team and free news site nine.com.au also facing cuts. Nine is announcing job cuts at The Sydney Morning Herald and The Age. Paul Rovere Managing director of the publishing division Tory Maguire informed staff of the upcoming changes in an email and town hall meeting on Tuesday morning, saying that the business needed to adapt to a changing media landscape to protect its journalism. This includes investing in new areas to connect with new audiences, she said. “In particular we need to keep shifting towards the digital-first future,” Maguire said, though she added that print revenue has held up better than previously expected.

Advertisement

Maguire said the company would engage with the Media, Entertainment and Arts Alliance (MEAA) union that represents journalists. Related Article Exclusive

Media & marketing Up to 200 staff to go as Seven’s owner swings the axe Nine signed a landmark deal with Microsoft earlier this month, meaning the tech giant’s AI chatbot Copilot will reference content from the company’s mastheads in its search results, with the media company compensated in return. A briefing from investment bank UBS this month said revenue impact from the Microsoft deal would not have a major impact on the company’s bottom line, suggesting it is worth less than $25 million, but said it was “positive for future potential deals” ahead of expected legislation to bring tech giants to the bargaining table. News organisations around the world are adapting to the rapid uptake of AI chatbots as a tool for consuming information. Google, for example, has rolled out its AI Overview function over the past two years at the top of its search results, leading to a decline in click-through traffic both to news websites and all other web domains. Media union representative Cassie Derrick said the cuts were devastating and called on Nine to give more information about the cuts to the MEAA.

Advertisement

“Nine must engage in genuine consultation with staff through their union, and do everything possible to avoid forced redundancies, including redeployment and voluntary options,” Derrick said. “Newsrooms cannot keep absorbing job cuts without consequences for workloads, diversity, quality journalism and the ability of journalists to hold power to account.” The Australian Financial Review will not be affected by the cuts. Publishing was Nine’s second best-performing division in its 2025 full-year results, with revenue of $526 million and earnings, before a common range of deductions, of $153 million. Declines to print sales were offset by subscriber growth, the company said in its annual report last year. Projections show the division’s earnings are expected to be flat in the upcoming 2026 results, delivered in August. The company took $33 million in costs out of the division in the 2025 financial year. Nine has since sold its share of digital real estate business Domain for $1.4 billion, as well as its talkback radio division. It also purchased outdoor media company QMS for $850 million. title: Trump wants to make his trade wall bigger and better than ever before
text:
Advertisement

BusinessMarketsTrade wars Opinion Trump wants to make his trade wall bigger and better than ever before Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist July 21, 2026 — 12:04pm

July 21, 2026 — 12:04pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

As Donald Trump’s latest attempt to rebuild the tariff wall demolished by the US Supreme Court earlier this year goes live, he’s already planning to make it higher. This was always going to be a significant week for global trade, given that Trump’s temporary replacements for the “Liberation Day” tariffs – the 10 per cent baseline tariffs on almost all imports to the US, which the Supreme Court had ruled illegal in February – are scheduled to expire on Friday. Those replacement tariffs, using Section 122 of the Trade Act, have themselves been ruled illegal by US courts and the revenue raised may have to be reimbursed. In any event, they could only be imposed for a maximum of 150 days. US President Donald Trump is working to build a tariff wall the likes of which the world has never seen before. Bloomberg That period ends on Friday, and the Trump administration has been racing to get a more permanent replacement in place.

Advertisement

Last month, using a more robust section of the Trade Act, Section 301 (although it, too, might be challenged in the courts) the administration announced it would impose new tariffs of either 10 per cent or 12.5 per cent on 99.4 per cent of all imports to the US, using the pretext that it was a response to inadequate efforts by its trading partners, including Australia, to prevent imports made with forced labour. Separately, it identified 16 of America’s biggest trading partners – including China, Japan, the European Union, India and South Korea – as potential targets for even higher tariffs, claiming they had excess manufacturing capacity and production that burdened or restricted US commerce. Trump’s flurry of trade-related actions over the past week shows how committed he is to his misconceived understanding of trade. Last Friday, Trump announced even more tariffs, this time on Brazil, as retaliation for “unfair” trade practices, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio saying Brazil hadn’t negotiated in good faith and that President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva was “putting his own ego ahead of making a deal”, which is a bit rich coming from the Trump administration. Trump originally hit Brazil with 50 per cent tariffs as retaliation for the prosecution of former Brazilian president Jair Bolsonaro, a Trump friend and ally. Those were knocked out by the Supreme Court.

Advertisement

It is revealing – and shows the limitations of Trump’s trade wars – that the latest tariffs will only cover about a third of Brazil’s exports to the US, exempting coffee, orange juice, beef, nuts, honey, tropical fruit, iron and manganese ore, wood pulp, oil and oil products and civil aircraft, among a long list. Related Article Pollution Trump blames Canada for wildfire smoke as US forests also burn The initial tariffs, while they were in place, sent the price of coffee and other groceries soaring in the US, causing a consumer backlash. The administration is clearly trying to avoid a repeat of that experience. There are some things that the US doesn’t or can’t produce itself, or where it can’t produce enough to meet its domestic demand, or where other countries can produce it more cheaply – something Trump has consistently failed to recognise in his trade wars with the rest of the world. As if the imminent introduction of the Section 301 tariffs, and the renewed but relatively limited attack on Brazil’s exports weren’t sufficient, on Monday Trump announced new 50 per cent tariffs on Canada, to take effect in 30 days.

Advertisement

Those tariffs are supposedly in response to the actions of Canadian provinces in halting purchases of US alcohol, putting tariffs on US cars and discriminating against US dairy products. Trump is, however, still seething over the fact that Canada was one of only two countries – China was the other – that retaliated when he imposed his Liberation Day tariffs. Last week, he even threatened another tariff because wildfires in Canada had resulted in the US being “invaded” by dirty air and pollution, saying the cost of the “pollution” should be added to the existing tariffs. The fact that Canada issued air quality warnings of its own, because of the smoke from US wildfires, seems to have escaped him. Donald Trump’s administration said Canada was one of only a few countries that had retaliated against US tariffs. AP Trump’s latest tariffs will apply to a wide range of Canadian exports, including those covered by the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA). In previous rounds of tariffs, goods complying with the free trade agreement were exempted.

Advertisement

Trump has, of course, given notice that the US will exit the USMCA (which he negotiated in 2018) when it expires in a decade, unless Mexico and Canada negotiate a new deal more favourable to the US. A third round of negotiations with Mexico is scheduled this week. So far, Canada has had no serious engagement with the US. Related Article Opinion

Trump's White House Trump’s three-pronged threat to American democracy Peter Hartcher Political and international editor The new tariffs could be a crude attempt to gain some leverage over Canada to force it to the table, although its Prime Minister Mark Carney has shown little interest in making concessions to the US. That the tariffs conflict with the USMCA could easily lead to a deeper trade conflict between the countries, even as Trump weighs up the prospect of reopening hostilities with the European Union.

Advertisement

Officials from his administration are putting together a list of Spanish goods the US could embargo in retaliation for Spain’s refusal to agree to spend 5 per cent of its GDP on defence, which Trump has insisted on. Spain’s government also stopped the US from using military bases within Spain to launch attacks on Iran. Earlier this month, at the NATO summit, Trump said he hadn’t spoken to Spain. “Spain is a wasted cause,” he said. “We don’t want to any trade business with Spain any more.” He also instructed US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to cut off trade with Spain. “Take care of ⁠it immediately. Don’t even talk to them. They’re hopeless. They’re bad people,” Trump said. “They make so much money with us, and we’re going to see that they make a lot less.” Trump tried to bask in the reflected glory of Spain’s World Cup success, but otherwise he’s not a fan of the country. Getty Images

Advertisement

The two countries actually don’t trade much with each other – the US imported about $US21 billion of Spanish goods last year and exported about $US26 billion to Spain, so it has a trade surplus with the country – but targeting Spain could blow up the deal the US finally signed with the EU late last month. The EU takes the view that a threat to one of its 27 members is a threat to all – so, if Trump’s threats against Spain are implemented, the wider trade deal could be jeopardised. The new tariffs, which may or may not survive the inevitable legal challenges from US importers, are Trump’s final attempt to rebuild his tariff wall through a patchwork of legislative headings that have never previously been used in the way that he proposes. Related Article Opinion

Trade wars Trump blows up the ‘best and most important deal ever made’ Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist The statutory authority for the new Canadian tariffs, for instance, is Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930, which essentially boils down to the preferential treatment Canada supposedly gives to third countries relative to its treatment of the US. It has never been used before.

Advertisement

It is conceivable that the Section 301 tariffs – which were intended, it seems, for use in bilateral disputes rather than to provide cover for a global tariff regime – could be demolished by the courts. It’s also possible that the Democrats gain control of Congress at the midterm elections and try to rein in Trump’s trade aggression. Trump’s flurry of trade-related actions over the past week, however, shows how committed he is to his misconceived understanding of trade. They also highlight his wilful ignorance of who eventually pays his tariffs and the reality that – despite his trade deals and promises that they would lead to massive investment in US manufacturing and a surge in well-paid manufacturing jobs – the manufacturing sector in the US continues to shrink, along with its jobs and real wages. title: ‘It’s people’s jobs’: Dragons take aim at costly refereeing calls
text:
Advertisement

SportNRLNRL 2026 ‘It’s people’s jobs’: Dragons take aim at costly refereeing calls Chris Barrett July 21, 2026 — 11:59am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

St George Illawarra have raised concerns with the NRL about a series of refereeing calls that have gone against them over the past month, with club chief Tim Watsford saying they had potential consequences “beyond the football field”. The last-placed Dragons have been far more competitive under Dean Young, who was on Monday handed the full-time head coaching job and a two-year contract. Dean Young was chosen ahead of 2010 Dragons premiership teammate Ben Hornby. Getty Images He was appointed despite the team being victorious in only two of 10 games under his tenure, a nod to the marked improvement they have made since he took over from Shane Flanagan in April following a winless start to the season. It was an acknowledgement from Dragons officials of Young’s “all-in” vision for the club and the confidence he had instilled in the line-up, but also a string of results impacted by refereeing.

Advertisement

Captain Damian Cook unloaded on the officiating after a 20-12 defeat to the Warriors on Saturday in which they were on the wrong side of three key decisions, querying whether their lowly position on the ladder worked against them. The Dragons also felt aggrieved at their treatment in close losses to Canberra and Newcastle, as well as the contentious denial of a solo try to Cook against Penrith at Magic Round. Loading “As a club, you’re kind of looked at by wins or losses; I’m not shying away from that, that’s the game we live in. But you could argue there’s three, possibly four games in Dean’s tenure that very much could have been a W next to it versus the L,” Watsford said. “That’s where pressure comes. This is beyond the football field a little bit; it’s got people’s jobs [at stake]. Dean’s been auditioning with some pretty ordinary calls. I called out a couple in Canberra and obviously on the weekend we saw a few, and there was a couple in Newcastle.”

Advertisement

Young beat long-time friend and 2010 premiership teammate Ben Hornby to the post in what Watsford indicated was a heavily subscribed application process. The Dragons CEO said Young was not chosen on the basis of wins and losses during the period in which he had been interim coach, but the club had written to the NRL repeatedly “to ensure that we’ve got our foot at the table”. The Dragons and their fans will be hoping for more success in 2027. Getty Images Young himself said he was more focused on Sunday’s match against 16th-placed Gold Coast Titans, which the Dragons would need to come out on top of to have any chance of avoiding the wooden spoon. As for next year, the club will welcome Keaon Koloamatangi, Scott Drinkwater, Connor Watson and Luke Metcalf, and Watsford made it clear that qualifying for the finals was a reasonable expectation.

Advertisement

Asked about making the top eight in 2027, Young said: “Look, with finals, [only] eight teams can make it ... so you’ve got to do a lot of things right. But if I don’t believe that we can make the finals next year, how are my players going to believe that? Related Article NRL 2026 Officials got three of seven calls wrong at the weekend. Apart from that, the NRL was happy “There’s a lot of work to be done, there’s a pre-season to be planned, but at the moment it’s this week against the Titans to make sure that we continue the consistency that we’ve shown in the last five or six weeks.” Young and father Craig have become one of a select few father-and-son combinations to be first-grade coaches of a club. Others include Don and David Furner with the Raiders, John and Stuart Raper with Cronulla, and Allan and Craig Fitzgibbon, also at the Sharks.

Advertisement

“It’s a very proud moment for myself, but also I understand the responsibility and the pressure that comes with this role,” he said. “I wanted to be a first-grade coach ever since I started back in 2013, and I’m thankful to the club to show the support and give me the opportunity. I’ve got two years to make the most of it, and that’s what I plan on doing.”

title: ‘Evasive’ postie who drove to KFC on lunch break loses job
text:
Advertisement

BusinessWorkplaceUnfair dismissal ‘Evasive’ postie who drove to KFC on lunch break loses job Millie Muroi July 21, 2026 — 11:47am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

A postie who drove his Australia Post truck to KFC on his lunch break without permission and admitted to being “evasive” about his whereabouts during work hours, has failed to win back his job after being sacked. The Newcastle man, who was employed by Australia Post for more than three decades before losing his job last year, said his dismissal was harsh, unjust and unreasonable, but was ultimately found to have engaged in serious and wilful misconduct by the country’s fair work umpire. A postal driver in NSW was dismissed after taking an Australia Post van to KFC on his lunch break without permission. AP That included being absent from duty for more than an hour on May 7 last year, and during that time, using an Australia Post vehicle for a non-work purpose – by his account, driving to KFC – without seeking permission and failing to tell his supervisor where he was or what he was doing. While he was entitled to a 30-minute unpaid lunch break, the driver did not take that break at the specified time of 10.40am. “[He] was required to let his supervisor know if he was taking a lunch break at a different time or location,” Fair Work Commission deputy president Tony Saunders said.

Advertisement

Related Article Unfair dismissal Chauffeur who agreed to retire at 65 to be paid $40,000 after ageist dismissal On the same day, the driver also left work 30 minutes early without seeking permission. Australia Post alleged the man was unreasonable, disrespectful, and acted inappropriately when asked by his supervisor to account for where he had been: first, refusing to speak, then telling his supervisor to “make an appointment” if speaking to him during work hours – a requirement Saunders said was unreasonable. When the driver refused to answer the question about his whereabouts, his supervisor asked: “why are you trying to be ...” While the supervisor had paused and was thinking about how to end the question, the driver offered, “evasive?” to which the supervisor responded: “yeah. That’s the perfect word.” Australia Post also alleged the driver had told his supervisor that he was “going to have some fun for the next two years.” When asked what he meant, the driver allegedly said: “I probably have another two years left before I retire.”

Advertisement

While the postie denied this exchange had occurred, the commission preferred Australia Post’s evidence on the alleged discussion, with Saunders saying the driver did “have some fun” by “playing games” with the company. Those games included replying to the company’s queries after hours when he knew the work phone was not being manned, and blocking text messages about his non-attendance. Australia Post also claimed the driver failed to attend work or to contact them regarding absences from work on at least 19 occasions over the course of a month, constituting repeated failures to follow reasonable directions. The Newcastle man was employed by Australia Post for more than three decades before losing his job last year. Jason South During that month, the man’s supervisor and several other managers sent about 17 text messages to his phone, on an almost daily basis because he had not attended work or said he would be absent, requesting him to call work to discuss the situation. “[The man’s] evidence in relation to these text messages was not credible,” Saunders said. “He said he thought the text messages might have been a scam, but ...[he] could not give a satisfactory explanation as to why he did not simply call his workplace to check whether the text messages were genuine.”

Advertisement

Related Article Exclusive

Workplace No hiring if an AI can do the job, staff at mining giant told The driver had been diagnosed as having “acute stress reaction” and had therefore been rostered on to work with reduced time pressure and lower volume. Medical certificates presented to the commission also showed he was suffering from mental health difficulties, which Saunders acknowledged had impacted the way in which the driver responded to the directions and requests from his employer. But Saunders said while the driver may have trouble finding an alternate job given his age and long employment history in one job, and would possibly face a difficult time from a financial perspective, his conduct was of “such a grave nature” as to be “repugnant” to the employment relationship. “A meaningful employment relationship cannot exist when an employee demonstrates an ongoing failure and refusal to co-operate with their employer,” he said. A spokesperson for Australia Post said the company welcomed the Fair Work Commission’s finding.



“We play an important role in delivering for the community and have high standards of conduct for all team members,” they said. The Business Briefing newsletter delivers major stories, exclusive coverage and expert opinion. Sign up to get it every weekday morning.

title: Scientists make dangerous discovery on ground-floor balconies in Aussie city
text:
Dust samples taken from outdoor areas around an Australian city have revealed high concentrations of toxic tyre-related pollution.

University of Queensland researchers tested balconies in inner-city and suburban areas of Brisbane and identified two factors leading to higher levels of exposure:

Balconies closer to the ground. Homes built nearer to the city.

Between 2024 and 2025, the team identified tyre additive chemicals in 29 of 30 samples, with some suspected to be dangerous to human health.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Worryingly, 6PPD-quinone was detected on 23 balconies.

This anti-degradant has caused mass salmon mortalities in the United States, and high concentrations have been found in Cubberla Creek, which flows into the Brisbane River.

In general, tyre wear particles (TWPs) were higher in areas closer to the Brisbane CBD. (ACS ES&T Air/University of Queensland)

PhD candidate Simran Kaur said his research provided the first evidence of tyre pollution on the balconies of Australian homes.

"These findings indicate outdoor residential dust may be an important 'sink' for 6PPD-quinone, with implications for human exposure," he said.

Separate research has previously shown that homes closer to major roads have higher concentrations of lead in the soil

Unlikely source of plastic exposure revealed

Dr Cassandra Rauert from the university's Queensland Alliance for Environmental Health Sciences said most Australians see tyre wear as a "normal part of driving".

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

But tyres are made from a cocktail of components, including natural rubber, plastics and chemical additives.

And during normal use, they shed tiny particles that can enter the environment as microplastics, polluting the environment.

Globally, around six million tonnes of tyre wear particles are released every year, with those that contain additives designed to resist degradation persisting the longest.

It's believed rain and wind are major causes of their spread, with strong gusts thought to deposit them on balconies.

RELATED:

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Driving is believed to be the biggest contributor of microplastics in outdoor spaces in urban settings, while inside, it's the shedding of textiles.

"The material does not simply disappear, the particles and chemicals are released into our environment and can travel beyond the road," Dr Rauert said.

The research has been published in the journal ACS ES&T Air.
